Rodighiero et al. (2026) — EGS-z11-R0: a red, dust-rich galaxy at Cosmic Dawn

EGS-z11-R0: a red, dust-rich galaxy at Cosmic Dawn

Paper Details

Authors: Giulia Rodighiero, Andrea Ferrara, Michele Catone, Lorenzo Napolitano, Paolo Cassata, Giovanni Gandolfi, Emiliano Merlin, Andrea Grazian, Alvio Renzini, et al.
arXiv: 2603.15841
Submitted: 2026-03-16
Journal: Submitted to A&A
Type: Observational — spectroscopy & multi-wavelength photometry
Instruments: JWST/NIRSpec (PRISM, G395M), JWST/NIRCam, JWST/MIRI, HST/ACS, HST/WFC3
Key concepts: Red monsters
Cosmic dawn
High-redshift galaxies
Interstellar dust
Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • EGS-z11-R0 at zspec = 11.452 ± 0.021 is the most distant spectroscopically confirmed dust-reddened galaxy, with UV slope βUV ≈ −1.0 lying far above the canonical MUV–βUV relation at z ~ 10–12, requiring dust attenuation AV ~ 1.0–1.3 mag[1][2].
  • Detection of [Fe V] λ4227 emission (S/N ~ 6) provides direct spectroscopic evidence for iron-peak element enrichment at z > 11, linking rapid chemical enrichment to dust production in the early ISM[1][3].
  • CIGALE SED modeling yields stellar mass log(M*/M⊙) ~ 9.2–9.6 and SFR ~ 10–40 M⊙ yr−1 with a rising or constant star formation history, indicating sustained stellar mass growth rather than a short-lived burst[1].
  • UV emission-line diagnostics (C IV/C III] ratios, equivalent widths) place EGS-z11-R0 in the composite AGN/SF region, consistent with a hard ionizing spectrum from extreme star formation and a possible moderate (~30%) AGN contribution[1][4].
  • The compact morphology (Reff < 280 pc), significant dust attenuation, and rising SFH are consistent with the Ferrara+2024 “red monster” scenario: a short-lived dust-obscured phase during which the bulk of stellar mass assembles before radiation-driven outflows clear the dust[1][5].

Implications

The spectroscopic confirmation of a dust-enriched, chemically evolved galaxy at z = 11.45 demonstrates that the earliest galaxies were not uniformly blue and dust-poor. Significant dust reservoirs and iron-peak enrichment existed only ~400 Myr after the Big Bang, challenging models that predict slow ISM grain growth and suggesting alternative rapid dust production channels (stellar sources, fast grain growth in dense environments). If “red monsters” represent ~20% of early galaxy lifetimes as predicted by Ferrara+2024, a non-negligible fraction of z > 10 star formation may be occurring in dust-obscured environments invisible to rest-frame UV surveys — with direct implications for cosmic star formation rate density estimates and reionization models.

Other works

EGS-z11-R0 fits within the emerging picture painted by JWST of a diverse high-redshift galaxy population. The red monster/blue monster framework of Ferrara+2024[5] predicted that early galaxies undergo a short-lived dust-enshrouded assembly phase, and EGS-z11-R0 provides the highest-redshift spectroscopic support for this model. Photometric candidates at z ~ 12 from Mitsuhashi+2025[6] and Donnan+2025[7] had already suggested the existence of this population, while Nakane+2025[3] showed that iron enrichment can occur very rapidly at z = 9–12. The detection of [Fe V] in EGS-z11-R0 complements the growing catalog of iron-line detections in high-z sources (Lambrides+2025[8]; PPG+2026[9]). The composite AGN/SF spectrum mirrors the diagnostic ambiguity seen in GN-z11[10] and GHZ9[11], underscoring the challenge of separating ionization sources at cosmic dawn.

Summary

Rodighiero et al. (2026) present the spectroscopic confirmation of EGS-z11-R0 at zspec = 11.452 ± 0.021, the most distant dust-reddened galaxy identified to date. Discovered serendipitously through inspection of publicly available JWST/NIRSpec data from the Dawn JWST Archive in the CEERS field, the source exhibits a remarkably red UV continuum slope (βUV ≈ −1.0), placing it well above the canonical MUV–βUV relation at z ~ 10–12 and making it the highest-redshift spectroscopically confirmed member of the emerging “red monster” population[1][2].

The redshift is determined from significant detections of C IV λλ1548,1551 (S/N = 3.9) and C III] λ1908 (S/N = 3.2) in the NIRSpec PRISM spectrum. SED modeling with CIGALE, combining spectroscopy and HST, NIRCam, and MIRI photometry, yields stellar mass log(M*/M⊙) ~ 9.2–9.6, SFR ~ 10–40 M⊙ yr−1, and substantial dust attenuation (AV ~ 1.2 mag). The source is unresolved in all NIRCam bands with effective radius upper limit of 177–280 pc[1].

A tentative detection of [Fe V] λ4227 emission in the G395M grism spectrum provides direct spectroscopic evidence for iron enrichment at z ~ 11.5, linking rapid chemical enrichment to dust grain production. The authors argue that EGS-z11-R0 represents a galaxy caught during the short-lived dust-obscured assembly phase predicted by Ferrara+2024[5], during which rapid star formation proceeds in a dust-enshrouded compact environment before radiation-driven outflows eventually disperse the dust[1].

Key Findings

Red UV continuum at z = 11.45

EGS-z11-R0 exhibits a UV slope βUV = −0.68 ± 0.30 (spectroscopic) and βUV = −1.20 ± 0.28 (photometric), both far redder than the typical βUV < −2.5 observed in z > 10 galaxies[1][2]. The red slope places the source significantly above the canonical MUV–βUV relation at z ~ 10–12, overlapping with the photometric red candidates identified by Mitsuhashi+2025[6]. Two physical mechanisms can explain such red slopes: (i) significant dust attenuation (AV ~ 0.5–1 mag) — the most direct explanation — or (ii) strong nebular continuum emission under extreme conditions (nH > 104 cm−3, Teff > 5 × 104 K)[6][12].

Iron enrichment at cosmic dawn

The detection of high-ionization forbidden line [Fe V] λ4227 at 5.2463 μm (S/N ~ 6) in the G395M spectrum represents one of the highest-redshift detections of collisionally excited iron emission to date[1]. Ionization of Fe4+ requires photons with energy > 54 eV, implying a hard radiation field difficult to produce with standard stellar populations alone[1]. Recent JWST studies show stellar iron abundances spanning [Fe/H] from ~−1 to solar at z = 9–12[3], and iron is promptly released by massive stars within ~10 Myr. The detection provides a chemical anchor linking the metals in the ISM to dust grain production — iron is a key constituent of silicate and oxide dust grains[1].

Composite ionizing spectrum

UV diagnostic diagrams (C IV/C III] vs. C III]/He II line ratios) place EGS-z11-R0 in the composite region, consistent with both AGN and extreme stellar ionizing spectra[1][4]. Large equivalent widths (EW(C IV) ~ 95 Å, EW(C III]) ~ 47 Å) combined with elevated metal-to-He II ratios require a hard ionizing spectrum[1]. Pure stellar photoionization models can reproduce such EWs only under extreme conditions (very low metallicity and log U > −2), while AGN models naturally predict strong high-ionization features[4][13]. The diagnostic degeneracy mirrors what is seen in other z > 10 systems like GN-z11[10] and GHZ9[11].

SED decomposition and physical properties

CIGALE SED modeling with HST, JWST/NIRCam, and JWST/MIRI photometry yields physical parameters that depend on assumed AGN fraction[1]. With no AGN contribution: log(M*/M⊙) = 9.64 ± 0.13 and SFR ~ 30–40 M⊙ yr−1. With moderate AGN fraction (fAGN = 0.3, compatible with observations): log(M*/M⊙) ~ 9.2 and SFR ~ 10–20 M⊙ yr−1. A dominant AGN fraction (fAGN = 0.6) is disfavored as the Fritz torus model exceeds MIRI/F1000W upper limits[1]. All solutions require substantial stellar reddening (E(B−V)* ~ 0.25–0.3 mag), confirming dust attenuation as the primary driver of the red UV slope. The best-fit dust luminosity LIR = (4.80 ± 1.20) × 1011 L⊙ supports interpretation as an obscured star-forming system[1].

Red monster scenario

The observational properties of EGS-z11-R0 — significant dust attenuation, compact morphology, rising or constant star formation history, and high stellar mass — match the predictions of the Ferrara+2024[5] radiative-feedback model. In this framework, early galaxies undergo a short-lived “red monster” phase lasting ~20% of their lifetime, during which ~70% of stellar mass forms in a dust-enshrouded environment. Radiation pressure from the growing stellar population eventually drives outflows that expel gas and dust, revealing the UV-bright “blue monster” phase[5]. If EGS-z11-R0 hosts a moderate AGN radiating at the Eddington limit, the implied black hole mass MBH ~ 3 × 105 M⊙ falls within the range expected for massive seed channels such as direct-collapse black holes or primordial black holes[1][14].

Methodology

EGS-z11-R0 was discovered through visual inspection of publicly available JWST/NIRSpec data from the Dawn JWST Archive (DJA)[15] during a systematic search for z > 10 candidates with red UV continua in the CEERS field[16]. NIRSpec observations include low-resolution PRISM spectroscopy (R ~ 100, 13130 sec exposure) covering the full 0.6–5.3 μm range, and medium-resolution G395M spectroscopy (R ~ 1000, 3130 sec) covering 2.87–5.14 μm[1]. Emission-line fitting used Gaussian profiles with MCMC analysis via emcee[17]. UV slope was measured by power-law fitting to the rest-frame 1350–2600 Å continuum[1]. SED modeling was performed with CIGALE[18] using BC03 stellar populations[19], stochastic star formation histories, Calzetti dust attenuation[20], and both Dale2014[21] and Fritz2006[22] AGN templates. Morphological analysis used GALFIT with NIRCam PSFs from STPSF[1].

Literature Context

JWST has revealed an abundant population of luminous galaxies at z > 10[23][24], predominantly characterized by extremely blue UV slopes (βUV < −2.5) consistent with young, metal-poor stellar populations with minimal dust[25][2]. Against this backdrop, a small population of red UV continuum candidates at z > 10 has emerged[26][6][7], deviating from the canonical MUV–βUV relation. The theoretical framework for interpreting these objects was provided by Ferrara+2024[5], who predicted that early galaxies undergo alternating dust-obscured (“red monster”) and dust-free (“blue monster”) phases regulated by radiative feedback. Alternative models include the feedback-free scenario of Dekel+2023[27] and density-regulated star formation models of Somerville+2025[28].

Prior to EGS-z11-R0, spectroscopic confirmation of dust-reddened systems at z > 10 remained largely absent. GHZ9 at z = 10.145[11] provided a lower-redshift analogue with similar properties (comparable stellar mass, ionization, dust content, and X-ray detection implying MBH ~ 108 M⊙). The detection of iron emission lines at high redshift connects to growing evidence from Nakane+2025[3] and Lambrides+2025[8] that iron enrichment occurs rapidly in early galaxies.

What This Paper Supports

  • Ferrara+2024 red monster/blue monster model: Rising SFH, compact morphology, and substantial dust attenuation all match predictions for the dust-enshrouded assembly phase[5].
  • Rapid chemical enrichment at cosmic dawn: [Fe V] detection confirms iron-peak enrichment at z > 11, consistent with prompt iron release from massive stars within ~10 Myr[3].
  • Diverse galaxy populations at z > 10: Alongside blue galaxies, the existence of red, dust-rich systems supports a heterogeneous picture of early galaxy evolution[1][6][7].
  • High stellar masses at z > 10: log(M*/M⊙) ~ 9.2–9.6 supports rapid early assembly seen in Santini+2025[29].

What This Paper Challenges

  • Slow ISM grain growth models: AV ~ 1 mag at z = 11.45 challenges models predicting bulk dust mass growth through ISM grain accretion requires high metallicities and extended timescales, implying heavily obscured galaxies should be extremely rare at z > 10[30].
  • Uniformly blue z > 10 populations: The canonical MUV–βUV relation at z ~ 10–12 predicts very blue slopes; EGS-z11-R0 lies significantly off this relation, suggesting the relation may underrepresent true UV slope diversity[2].
  • UV-based cosmic SFRD estimates: If red monsters are common (~20% of lifetime), rest-frame UV surveys may systematically undercount star formation at z > 10[1][5].

Keim et al. (2026) — A Third Galaxy Missing Dark Matter along a Trail of Galaxies in the NGC 1052 Field

A Third Galaxy Missing Dark Matter along a Trail of Galaxies in the NGC 1052 Field

Paper Details

Authors: Matthew A. Keim, Pieter van Dokkum, Zili Shen, Shany Danieli, et al.
arXiv: 2603.15860
Submitted: 2026-03-16
Journal: Submitted
Type: Observational — stellar kinematics spectroscopy
Instruments: Keck/KCWI, Keck/LRIS, HST/ACS
Key concepts: Dark-matter-free galaxies
Bullet dwarf scenario
Ultra-diffuse galaxies
Stellar mass–halo mass relation
Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • DF9's stellar velocity dispersion (σ = 6.4+4.0−4.3 km/s) confirms it lacks dark matter, consistent with the 8.3 km/s expected from its 1.4 × 108 M⊙ stellar mass alone and far below the 27 ± 3 km/s expected from a standard NFW dark matter halo [1][2].
  • Three galaxies on the NGC 1052 trail are now confirmed dark-matter-free (DF2, DF4, DF9), all falling below the universal baryon fraction line — ruling out normal scatter in the stellar mass–halo mass relation [3][4].
  • The bullet dwarf collision scenario made a falsifiable prediction that trail galaxies should lack dark matter, and DF9 validates this prediction [5][6][7].
  • Systematic corrections for stellar broadening (σbroadening = 5.4 ± 2 km/s) and binary orbital motion (σbinaries = 2.3 ± 0.6 km/s) are essential for low-dispersion systems, reducing the measured 8.7 km/s to 6.4 km/s [8].
  • Star cluster velocities provide an independent consistency check: both DF9 clusters show velocities very close to the diffuse light systemic velocity, consistent with a low-mass system [9].

Implications

This paper strengthens the case that the NGC 1052 trail represents a fundamentally different galaxy formation channel — one where dark matter and baryons were physically separated by a high-speed collision. The confirmation of a third dark-matter-free galaxy transforms what was a two-object curiosity into a systematic pattern, making it much harder to dismiss as measurement error or statistical fluke. For galaxy evolution, this demonstrates that the assumed universality of the dark matter halo framework breaks down in specific extreme environments, with implications for how we interpret the faintest galaxies and scatter in scaling relations.

Other works

Keim et al. (2026) builds directly on the discoveries of DF2 and DF4 (van Dokkum et al. 2018 [3]; van Dokkum et al. 2019 [4]) and the bullet dwarf collision model (Silk 2019 [5]; Shin et al. 2020 [6]; Lee et al. 2021 [10], 2024 [7]). It supports the growing recognition that ultra-diffuse galaxies span an enormous range of dark matter content. The work challenges the standard bottom-up galaxy formation paradigm by providing a concrete example of top-down formation from collision debris, connecting to broader questions about the nature of dark matter through the demonstrated physical separability of dark and baryonic matter.

Summary

Keim et al. (2026) present Keck/KCWI spectroscopy of NGC 1052-DF9, a ultra-diffuse galaxy in the NGC 1052 field that is part of a remarkable linear trail of ~12 faint galaxies kinematically connected to each other [11]. The measured stellar velocity dispersion of σ = 6.4+4.0−4.3 km/s is consistent with the prediction from stellar mass alone (8.3 km/s) but far below the 27 ± 3 km/s expected if DF9 inhabited a standard dark matter halo following the stellar mass–halo mass relation [1][2].

This makes DF9 the third galaxy in the NGC 1052 trail confirmed to lack dark matter, after DF2 [3] and DF4 [4]. All three galaxies fall below the universal baryon fraction line, meaning any residual dark matter halo would need an unphysically high baryon fraction [1]. The result validates a specific, falsifiable prediction of the bullet dwarf scenario, in which a high-speed collision between two gas-rich galaxies separated baryons from dark matter, producing a trail of dark-matter-free dwarf galaxies [5][6].

The paper also carefully accounts for systematic effects in low-dispersion integrated-light measurements, including stellar rotation/macroturbulence and binary star orbital motion, which contribute broadening comparable to the gravitational dispersion itself [8].

Key Findings

DF9 velocity dispersion and dark matter content

From 10.83 hours of Keck/KCWI integration, Keim et al. measure a fitted velocity dispersion of σfit = 8.7+3.4−3.7 km/s using MCMC template fitting with MIST isochrone synthetic spectra [1][12]. After subtracting stellar broadening (5.4 ± 2 km/s) and binary orbital motion (2.3 ± 0.6 km/s) in quadrature, the gravitational velocity dispersion is σ = 6.4+4.0−4.3 km/s [1][8]. The dynamical mass within the effective radius is Me = 0.42+0.68−0.37 × 108 M⊙, consistent with the stellar mass within that radius (0.71 ± 0.16 × 108 M⊙) [1][13].

Comparison to dark matter halo predictions

Using the SMHM relation from Zahra et al. (2023) [2] with an NFW halo profile [14], the expected velocity dispersion for DF9 would be 27 ± 3 km/s — highly inconsistent with the observed 6.4 km/s. Alternative SMHM relations yield even higher predictions (~32 km/s) [15]. DF9 joins DF2 and DF4 as extreme outliers in the re–σ–M☆ plane: galaxies with similar dispersions are several times smaller, while galaxies with similar stellar masses have much larger dispersions [1].

Trail of dark-matter-free galaxies

DF2, DF4, and DF9 are all members of a linear alignment of ~12 faint galaxies in the NGC 1052 field, with velocities increasing linearly with position along the trail [11][9]. All three fall below the universal baryon fraction in the dynamical mass vs. stellar mass comparison, requiring either extremely small dark matter halos with unphysical baryon fractions, or complete absence of dark matter [1][16].

Dispute: Survivability of dark-matter-free galaxies

Challenge: Ogiya (2022) [17] questioned whether dark-matter-free galaxies could survive tidal disruption in the NGC 1052 group environment, arguing they would be destroyed on short timescales.

Response: Lee et al. (2024) [7] performed dedicated simulations of the bullet dwarf scenario specific to the NGC 1052 system, demonstrating that trail galaxies can survive under the actual tidal field conditions. The existence of DF9 with properties consistent with DF2/DF4 provides empirical evidence for survivability.

Bullet dwarf scenario confirmation

The bullet dwarf scenario — a high-speed collision between gas-rich dwarf galaxies that separates baryons from dark matter [5] — predicted the trail before its discovery [6] and predicted that trail galaxies should lack dark matter. DF9 confirms this prediction [1]. The scenario also explains the extremely luminous globular clusters [10], large galaxy sizes [18], and nearly monochromatic cluster colors [19] observed in trail galaxies.

Methodology

Observations were obtained with Keck/KCWI over two nights (2024 Oct 2–3) using the medium image slicer, RH3 grating at 8600Å central wavelength, and 2×2 binning [1]. Total integration was 10.83 hours (24 on-target, 28 sky exposures of 750s each). Custom cosmic ray rejection adapted from CRCombine was applied, and sky subtraction used neighboring sky frames rescaled to match sky line strength [1]. Template fitting used 12 synthetic spectra from MIST isochrones [12] spanning metallicities [−1.5, −1.25, −1.0] and ages [7, 8, 9, 10] Gyr, with MCMC sampling via emcee [20]. Instrumental resolution was measured at σinst = 0.375Å (13.0 km/s) [1]. Star cluster velocities from prior Keck/LRIS observations [9] provided an independent consistency check.

Literature Context

The standard paradigm of galaxy formation holds that galaxies form within dark matter halos, with low-mass dwarfs being the most dark-matter-dominated systems (halo masses >100× stellar mass) [16][21]. DF2 (van Dokkum et al. 2018 [3]) and DF4 (van Dokkum et al. 2019 [4]) challenged this paradigm as the first galaxies with dynamical masses consistent with stellar masses alone. The discovery of the linear trail of galaxies in the NGC 1052 field [11] and its kinematic connection [9] provided the context for the bullet dwarf collision model [5], which uniquely explains the full suite of unusual properties: dark matter deficiency, luminous globular clusters, large sizes, and linear alignment.

What This Paper Supports

  • Bullet dwarf collision scenario: Validates the specific prediction that trail galaxies should lack dark matter [5][6][7].
  • Dark-matter-free galaxy class: Establishes a third member, transforming a two-object curiosity into a systematic pattern [1].
  • Physical separability of dark and baryonic matter: Confirms that dark matter behaves as a collisionless component that can be separated from gas during high-speed encounters [5][22].
  • Common origin of trail galaxies: Kinematic connection and shared dark matter deficiency support formation in a single event [11][9].

What This Paper Challenges

  • Universality of dark matter halos: The standard assumption that all galaxies form within dark matter halos fails for DF2/DF4/DF9 [16][21].
  • SMHM relation applicability to all dwarfs: DF9 is a >5σ outlier from the predicted dispersion [2].
  • Alternative formation models: Tidal dwarf [23], quasar-driven outflow [24], and tidal stripping [25] scenarios cannot explain the full combination of trail alignment, luminous GCs, and dark matter deficiency [1].

Spatially resolved stellar populations and emission lines in the M51 group with J-PLUS

Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • The M51 group shows signatures of more advanced dynamical evolution than the M101 group, with flattened age–Σ★ and nebular metallicity–Σ★ relations, enhanced dust content, and environmental quenching in some members[1].
  • The interacting pair M51a/b displays sharply distinct properties: M51a has prominent star-forming spiral arms with young populations, while M51b is a fully retired early-type galaxy — a clean example of interaction-driven divergence within a single system[1].
  • M63 exhibits outside-in quenching signatures (asymmetric stellar age, dust attenuation, and Hα EW maps), likely driven by a past interaction rather than classical inside-out evolution[1].
  • All star-forming galaxies in both groups follow the resolved star-forming main sequence, with M51b the sole exception as a retired system[1][2].
  • J-PLUS 12-band photometric datacubes processed with AlStar can deliver IFS-like spatially resolved stellar population and emission-line maps for nearby galaxies[1][3].

Implications

This paper demonstrates that even low-mass galaxy groups can exhibit significant environmental processing of their members, challenging the view that such effects are limited to massive clusters. The contrast between M51 (interaction-processed, flattened gradients, quenched members) and M101 (largely pristine inside-out formation) provides a compelling case study for how group dynamics shape galaxy evolution. The J-PLUS methodology opens a path to extending IFS-like analyses across much larger galaxy samples than traditional integral field surveys.

Other works

This study builds on the CALIFA IFS results (González Delgado et al. 2014, 2015, 2016[4][5][6]; García-Benito et al. 2017[7]) that established spatially resolved scaling relations for stellar populations. The environmental dependence connects to Kauffmann et al. (2004)[8] and the quenching literature (Bluck et al. 2020[9]). The M51 system has been extensively studied (Dobbs et al. 2010[10]; Mentuch Cooper et al. 2012[11]), but this is the first uniform multi-band resolved analysis of the entire group compared to another (M101; Thainá-Batista et al. 2023[12]). The resolved SFMS framework follows Sánchez et al. (2020)[2], while outside-in quenching adds to evidence for diverse quenching pathways (Tacchella et al. 2015[13]; Nelson et al. 2021[14]).

Summary

Thainá-Batista et al. (2026) characterize the spatially resolved stellar population and emission-line properties of galaxies in the M51 group using multi-band datacubes from the Javalambre Photometric Local Universe Survey (J-PLUS). The same AlStar spectral fitting methodology previously applied to the M101 group[12] is used, enabling a direct and fair comparison between two nearby galaxy groups with different interaction histories[1].

The M51 group displays clear signatures of environmental processing: flattened age and nebular metallicity gradients, enhanced dust content, and quenching in some members. The interacting pair M51a/b shows dramatic property contrasts — star-forming spiral arms in M51a versus a retired companion in M51b. M63 shows asymmetric quenching consistent with a past interaction. In contrast, the M101 group largely preserves the steep radial gradients expected from undisturbed inside-out galaxy formation[1].

The study highlights J-PLUS's potential for IFS-like analyses of nearby galaxies and suggests that group mass and interactions influence galaxy evolution even in low-mass environments, though the comparison of only two systems limits generalizability[1].

Key Findings

M51a/b: Interaction-driven property divergence

The interacting pair M51a (NGC 5194) and M51b (NGC 5195) displays clearly distinct properties in all resolved maps[1]. M51a shows prominent star-forming spiral arms with young stellar populations, high Hα equivalent widths, and enhanced dust attenuation. M51b, despite being physically connected to M51a, is essentially an early-type retired galaxy with old stellar populations and negligible current star formation[1]. This pair provides a textbook example of how galaxy interactions can produce divergent evolutionary outcomes.

M63: Outside-in quenching

M63 (NGC 5055) exhibits asymmetries in stellar age, dust attenuation, and Hα equivalent width that are consistent with outside-in quenching[1]. This pattern — where the outer regions quench before the inner disk — is the opposite of classical inside-out formation and likely relates to a past interaction event. This finding adds to evidence that quenching pathways are diverse and environment-dependent[13][14].

Flattened scaling relations in the M51 group

The age–Σ★ and nebular metallicity–Σ★ relations in the M51 group are flatter than in the M101 group[1]. This flattening is interpreted as evidence of more advanced dynamical evolution: interactions mix stellar populations and redistribute metals, erasing the steep gradients that reflect pristine inside-out disk growth. The M101 group, being less dynamically evolved, preserves these original gradients[1][12].

Resolved star-forming main sequence

All star-forming galaxies in both groups align with the resolved star-forming main sequence (RSFMS), confirming its universality at sub-galactic scales[1][2]. The exception is M51b, which falls below the RSFMS in the regime of retired galaxies. This demonstrates that the RSFMS holds even in group environments with active interactions[1].

Methodology

The study uses J-PLUS (Javalambre Photometric Local Universe Survey) data consisting of 12 photometric bands (7 narrowband + 5 broadband filters) covering 3500–10000 Å[15][16]. Multi-band datacubes are constructed for each galaxy and processed with the AlStar spectral fitting code, which fits stellar population models (Bruzual & Charlot 2003[17] with Chabrier 2003 IMF[18]) and Calzetti et al. (2000)[19] dust attenuation to each spatial element[1].

Emission lines (Hα, Hβ, [O III], [N II]) are extracted from narrowband filter residuals after stellar continuum subtraction. The BPT diagnostic diagram (Baldwin et al. 1981[20]) is used to classify ionization sources. Nebular metallicities are derived from strong-line calibrations[1]. The identical methodology was previously applied to the M101 group[12], ensuring a consistent comparison.

Literature Context

This work extends the CALIFA tradition of spatially resolved stellar population analysis[4][5][6][7] to photometric survey data, trading spectral resolution for spatial coverage. The M51 system is among the most studied interacting pairs (Dobbs et al. 2010[10]; Tress et al. 2020[21]), but this paper provides the first uniform comparison of its resolved properties against another group using identical methods.

The environmental dependence of galaxy properties in groups connects to a broad literature on quenching mechanisms, including Kauffmann et al. (2004)[8] on environmental modulation of the mass–SFR relation, Bluck et al. (2020)[9] on central velocity dispersion as a quenching predictor, and Tacchella et al. (2015)[13] on inside-out quenching. The study also contributes to growing interest in J-PLUS for extragalactic science (Rodríguez-Martín et al. 2025[3]; San Roman et al. 2019[22]).

What This Paper Supports

  • Resolved star-forming main sequence: Star-forming members of both groups follow the RSFMS at sub-galactic scales[1][2].
  • Inside-out formation (in undisturbed systems): The M101 group preserves steep age and metallicity gradients consistent with inside-out disk growth[1][4][5].
  • Galaxy interactions drive environmental processing: The M51 group's flattened gradients, enhanced dust, and quenched members result from tidal interactions[1][8].
  • Environmental quenching operates in low-mass groups: Not limited to massive clusters[1].

What This Paper Challenges

  • Universal inside-out formation: Interaction-processed groups show flattened gradients, indicating inside-out signatures can be erased by environment[1].
  • Mass-only quenching models: Stellar mass surface density alone does not predict quenching; interaction history matters even in low-mass groups[1][9].

O'Connor et al. (2026) — SN 2025adpq: A Type Ia supernova in a collisional ring formed during a major galaxy merger

SN 2025adpq: A Type Ia supernova in a collisional ring formed during a major galaxy merger

Paper Details

Authors: Brendan O'Connor, Xander J. Hall, Tomas Cabrera, Lei Hu, Antonella Palmese, Louis-Gregory Strolger, et al.
arXiv: 2603.15899
Submitted: 2026-03-16
Journal: To be submitted to The Open Journal of Astrophysics
Type: Observational — multi-wavelength photometry & spectroscopy
Instruments: DECam, SALT/RSS, Gemini/GMOS-S, KiDS/OmegaCAM, VIKING/VISTA
Key concepts: Type Ia supernovae
Collisional ring galaxies
Galaxy mergers
Supernova delay times
Hostless supernovae
Large-offset supernovae
Dynamical stellar displacement
Star formation
Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • SN 2025adpq is a normal Type Ia supernova at z = 0.1540 discovered at 11.4 kpc projected offset from its host galaxy G1, located within a collisional ring ("Pika's Halo") produced by a major merger between two ~1010.5 M☉ galaxies[1].
  • Spectroscopy reveals a dual stellar population in the ring: deep Ca H&K absorption indicates old stars displaced from G1, while bright emission lines ([O II], Hα) show ongoing merger-triggered star formation at SFR ~0.91 M☉/yr with subsolar metallicity (~0.6 Z☉)[2][3].
  • The SN Ia most likely arose from an old progenitor (delay time ≥0.5 Gyr) stripped from G1 by the collisional pressure wave, given that the ring is only ~70–200 Myr old while G1 formed most of its stars ~11 Gyr ago[4][5][6].
  • Collisional rings provide a new pathway for producing large-offset and apparently hostless Type Ia SNe by dynamically displacing long-lived progenitor systems from their parent galaxies[7][8][9].
  • A systematic crossmatch of low-z CRG catalogs with TNS reveals CRGs as "supernova factories": 15 transients in 11 CRG systems, 3 hosting supernova siblings, with SN 2025adpq being only the 2nd classified Type Ia in a CRG ring[10].
  • At higher redshifts, surface brightness dimming would render collisional rings undetectable, causing these offset SNe to be misclassified as hostless — a systematic bias that Rubin LSST will help resolve[11].

Implications

This paper establishes collisional ring galaxies as a previously unrecognized channel for producing large-offset Type Ia supernovae, with direct implications for galaxy evolution studies. The demonstration that galaxy-galaxy collisions can dynamically displace old stellar populations to tens of kpc from their birthplace challenges the assumption that SN Ia environments directly reflect their progenitor formation sites. For cosmological surveys using Type Ia SNe as standardizable candles, the systematic misclassification of CRG-offset SNe as "hostless" at higher redshifts could introduce biases in host-galaxy-dependent corrections. More broadly, the coexistence of old displaced stars and newly triggered star formation in collisional rings offers a unique laboratory for studying stellar population mixing, delay time distributions, and the interplay between gravitational dynamics and star formation during galaxy mergers.

Other works

This paper builds on the theoretical framework of collisional ring formation (Lynds & Toomre 1976[12]; Appleton & Struck-Marcell 1996[13]) and extends earlier observations of Type Ia SNe in tidal structures (Schweizer et al. 2008[14]; Ferretti et al. 2017[15]) to the specific context of collisionally-driven density waves. The proposed dynamical displacement mechanism connects to the broader "hostless SN Ia" problem (Gal-Yam et al. 2003[7]; Sand et al. 2011[16]; Graham et al. 2015[8]; Qin et al. 2024[17]), offering collisional rings as a complementary explanation alongside intracluster stars and ultra-diffuse dwarf hosts (Strolger et al. 2025[9]). The finding that CRGs are supernova factories aligns with studies showing elevated core-collapse SN rates in interacting galaxies (Habergham et al. 2010, 2012[18][19]; Michalowski et al. 2020[20]) and extends this to redistributed Type Ia progenitors (Ditrani et al. 2024[21]).

Summary

O'Connor et al. (2026) report the discovery and characterization of SN 2025adpq, a Type Ia supernova at z = 0.1540 found within a collisional ring galaxy system dubbed "Pika's Halo." The ring, with a circumference of ~70 kpc, was produced by a major merger between two galaxies of comparable mass (log(M*/M☉) ≈ 10.5)[1]. The supernova lies at a projected offset of ~11.4 kpc from the nucleus of the primary galaxy G1, along the expanding ring[1].

Multi-instrument spectroscopy with SALT/RSS and Gemini/GMOS-S reveals that the ring hosts both ongoing star formation (bright emission lines including [O II], [O III], Hβ, and Hα) and a substantial old stellar population (deep Ca H&K absorption)[1]. Given that Type Ia SN progenitors have delay times ≥0.5 Gyr[4][5] and the ring is estimated to be only ~70–200 Myr old, the authors argue that SN 2025adpq most likely originated from an old progenitor system that was dynamically displaced from G1 during the head-on collision[1].

Key Findings

Collisional ring identification and system properties

The system is confirmed as a major merger (luminosity ratio L2/L1 ~ 0.8) producing a collisional ring, not a gravitational lens[22]. Spectroscopy at consistent z ~ 0.1537–0.1540 for all components (SN, ring, G1, G2) rejects the lensing hypothesis[1]. The ring has a projected semi-major axis of 10.5 kpc and semi-minor axis of 9.4 kpc, implying an inclination of ~26.5°[1]. A coherent velocity structure with Δv ~ 350 km/s across a 70° position-angle span is observed, consistent with an expanding ring with some residual rotation[23].

Dual stellar populations in the ring

Spectroscopy reveals coexisting old and young stellar populations within the ring[1]. The Balmer decrement (Hα/Hβ = 2.65 ± 0.13) is consistent with Case B recombination, and BPT ratios confirm star-forming ionization[1]. Gas-phase metallicity is subsolar at 12 + log(O/H) ~ 8.50 ± 0.05 (~0.6 Z☉)[2]. SED modeling with Prospector confirms both galaxies formed the bulk of their stars at early times (~11 Gyr ago), with G1 SFR = 0.41 M☉/yr and G2 SFR = 0.10 M☉/yr[6][24].

SN classification and light curve

SN 2025adpq is classified as a normal Type Ia via NGSF and SNAIL template matching, with best spectral match to SN 2008C at 15.4 days post-peak[25][26]. SALT2 light-curve fitting yields peak time ~7.5 ± 1.1 d before discovery, stretch x1 = −0.81 ± 0.31, and colour c = 0.07 ± 0.03 (χ2/dof = 18.6/12)[27][28].

Ring age and dynamical displacement argument

The ring age is estimated at 70–200 Myr based on the ring radius (~10.5 kpc) and expansion velocities of 50–150 km/s[29][30]. This is far shorter than the ≥0.5 Gyr delay times expected for most Type Ia progenitors[4][5]. SED modeling shows G1 formed the bulk of its stars ~11 Gyr ago[6], consistent with an old delayed progenitor channel. The collisional pressure wave both triggered new star formation and swept out pre-existing disk stars, as evidenced by Ca H&K absorption in the ring[1].

CRGs as supernova factories

A crossmatch of the Madore et al. (2009) low-z CRG catalog[10] with TNS identifies 15 transients within 30 kpc of 11 CRG systems (8% of the sample), with 7 in rings and 8 not in rings. Three CRG systems host supernova siblings. NGC 4774 is a "SN factory" with 3 transients all within its ring[1]. SN 2025adpq is the 8th classified SN in a CRG ring and only the 2nd Type Ia (after SN 2025khv)[1].

Methodology

SN 2025adpq was discovered on 2025-11-14 at g = 20.5 AB mag with DECam during gravitational wave follow-up (GW-MMADS survey)[31]. Seven DECam epochs in griz filters spanning 2025-11-14 to 2025-12-31 were obtained for the light curve, processed with a GPU-enabled difference imaging pipeline using the SFFT algorithm[32][33]. Spectroscopic follow-up was performed with SALT/RSS (PA = 90°E) and Gemini/GMOS-S (PA = 129.28°)[34][35]. Archival imaging from KiDS (optical, ugri) and VIKING (NIR, YJHKs) provided the best pre-explosion views[36][37]. Stellar population properties were derived using pPXF spectral fitting[38] and Prospector SED modeling with nonparametric star formation histories[6].

Literature Context

Collisional ring galaxies were first theorized by Lynds & Toomre (1976)[12] as resulting from near head-on encounters that produce density waves propagating through disk galaxies. Only ~130 CRGs have been identified at low redshift (z ~ 0.002–0.09), representing ~1% of all ring galaxies[10]. Their short lifetimes (≤0.5 Gyr)[29][30][39] make them rare. Enhanced star formation in rings has been documented by multiple studies[40][41][30].

The paper sits at the intersection of two established fields: Type Ia supernova host environments[42][43][44] and the hostless SN Ia problem[7][8][17]. Previous work found Type Ia SNe in tidal structures[14][15], and Ditrani et al. (2024)[21] modeled the redistribution of old progenitors by interactions. This paper extends these findings to the specific context of collisional density waves.

What This Paper Supports

  • Star formation triggered by galaxy interactions: The SFR of ~0.91 M☉/yr in the collisional ring confirms that galaxy-scale dynamical events can drive substantial star formation in propagating density waves[40][41][30].
  • Long delay times for Type Ia progenitors: The mismatch between ring age (~70–200 Myr) and required SN Ia delay times (≥0.5 Gyr) strongly supports the old progenitor channel[4][5].
  • Type Ia SNe trace old stellar populations: Consistent with van den Bergh & Tammann (1991)[45] and Anderson et al. (2015)[46].
  • Enhanced core-collapse SN rates in interacting galaxies: The finding that CRGs are SN factories aligns with Habergham et al. (2010, 2012)[18][19].

What This Paper Challenges

  • SN Ia environments = progenitor birthplaces: The paper demonstrates that dynamical displacement can relocate old progenitors tens of kpc from their formation sites, decoupling environment from origin[1].
  • Simple "hostless" classification: Some apparently hostless SNe Ia at higher z may actually reside in low surface-brightness tidal or ring structures rendered invisible by cosmological dimming[1][9].

SN 2025adpq: A Type Ia supernova in a collisional ring formed during a major galaxy merger

Paper Details

Authors: Brendan O'Connor, Xander J. Hall, Tomas Cabrera, Lei Hu, Antonella Palmese, Louis-Gregory Strolger, et al.

Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • SN 2025adpq is a normal Type Ia supernova at z = 0.1540 discovered at 11.4 kpc projected offset from its host galaxy G1, located within a collisional ring ("Pika's Halo") produced by a major merger between two ~1010.5 M☉ galaxies[1].
  • Spectroscopy reveals a dual stellar population in the ring: deep Ca H&K absorption indicates old stars displaced from G1, while bright emission lines ([O II], Hα) show ongoing merger-triggered star formation at SFR ~0.91 M☉/yr with subsolar metallicity (~0.6 Z☉)[2][3].
  • The SN Ia most likely arose from an old progenitor (delay time ≥0.5 Gyr) stripped from G1 by the collisional pressure wave, given that the ring is only ~70–200 Myr old while G1 formed most of its stars ~11 Gyr ago[4][5][6].
  • Collisional rings provide a new pathway for producing large-offset and apparently hostless Type Ia SNe by dynamically displacing long-lived progenitor systems from their parent galaxies[7][8][9].
  • A systematic crossmatch of low-z CRG catalogs with TNS reveals CRGs as "supernova factories": 15 transients in 11 CRG systems, 3 hosting supernova siblings, with SN 2025adpq being only the 2nd classified Type Ia in a CRG ring[10].
  • At higher redshifts, surface brightness dimming would render collisional rings undetectable, causing these offset SNe to be misclassified as hostless — a systematic bias that Rubin LSST will help resolve[11].

Implications

This paper establishes collisional ring galaxies as a previously unrecognized channel for producing large-offset Type Ia supernovae, with direct implications for galaxy evolution studies. The demonstration that galaxy-galaxy collisions can dynamically displace old stellar populations to tens of kpc from their birthplace challenges the assumption that SN Ia environments directly reflect their progenitor formation sites. For cosmological surveys using Type Ia SNe as standardizable candles, the systematic misclassification of CRG-offset SNe as "hostless" at higher redshifts could introduce biases in host-galaxy-dependent corrections. More broadly, the coexistence of old displaced stars and newly triggered star formation in collisional rings offers a unique laboratory for studying stellar population mixing, delay time distributions, and the interplay between gravitational dynamics and star formation during galaxy mergers.

Other works

This paper builds on the theoretical framework of collisional ring formation (Lynds & Toomre 1976[12]; Appleton & Struck-Marcell 1996[13]) and extends earlier observations of Type Ia SNe in tidal structures (Schweizer et al. 2008[14]; Ferretti et al. 2017[15]) to the specific context of collisionally-driven density waves. The proposed dynamical displacement mechanism connects to the broader "hostless SN Ia" problem (Gal-Yam et al. 2003[7]; Sand et al. 2011[16]; Graham et al. 2015[8]; Qin et al. 2024[17]), offering collisional rings as a complementary explanation alongside intracluster stars and ultra-diffuse dwarf hosts (Strolger et al. 2025[9]). The finding that CRGs are supernova factories aligns with studies showing elevated core-collapse SN rates in interacting galaxies (Habergham et al. 2010, 2012[18][19]; Michalowski et al. 2020[20]) and extends this to redistributed Type Ia progenitors (Ditrani et al. 2024[21]).

Summary

O'Connor et al. (2026) report the discovery and characterization of SN 2025adpq, a Type Ia supernova at z = 0.1540 found within a collisional ring galaxy system dubbed "Pika's Halo." The ring, with a circumference of ~70 kpc, was produced by a major merger between two galaxies of comparable mass (log(M*/M☉) ≈ 10.5)[1]. The supernova lies at a projected offset of ~11.4 kpc from the nucleus of the primary galaxy G1, along the expanding ring[1].

Multi-instrument spectroscopy with SALT/RSS and Gemini/GMOS-S reveals that the ring hosts both ongoing star formation (bright emission lines including [O II], [O III], Hβ, and Hα) and a substantial old stellar population (deep Ca H&K absorption)[1]. Given that Type Ia SN progenitors have delay times ≥0.5 Gyr[4][5] and the ring is estimated to be only ~70–200 Myr old, the authors argue that SN 2025adpq most likely originated from an old progenitor system that was dynamically displaced from G1 during the head-on collision[1].

Key Findings

Collisional ring identification and system properties

The system is confirmed as a major merger (luminosity ratio L2/L1 ~ 0.8) producing a collisional ring, not a gravitational lens[22]. Spectroscopy at consistent z ~ 0.1537–0.1540 for all components (SN, ring, G1, G2) rejects the lensing hypothesis[1]. The ring has a projected semi-major axis of 10.5 kpc and semi-minor axis of 9.4 kpc, implying an inclination of ~26.5°[1]. A coherent velocity structure with Δv ~ 350 km/s across a 70° position-angle span is observed, consistent with an expanding ring with some residual rotation[23].

Dual stellar populations in the ring

Spectroscopy reveals coexisting old and young stellar populations within the ring[1]. The Balmer decrement (Hα/Hβ = 2.65 ± 0.13) is consistent with Case B recombination, and BPT ratios confirm star-forming ionization[1]. Gas-phase metallicity is subsolar at 12 + log(O/H) ~ 8.50 ± 0.05 (~0.6 Z☉)[2]. SED modeling with Prospector confirms both galaxies formed the bulk of their stars at early times (~11 Gyr ago), with G1 SFR = 0.41 M☉/yr and G2 SFR = 0.10 M☉/yr[6][24].

SN classification and light curve

SN 2025adpq is classified as a normal Type Ia via NGSF and SNAIL template matching, with best spectral match to SN 2008C at 15.4 days post-peak[25][26]. SALT2 light-curve fitting yields peak time ~7.5 ± 1.1 d before discovery, stretch x1 = −0.81 ± 0.31, and colour c = 0.07 ± 0.03 (χ2/dof = 18.6/12)[27][28].

SN 2025adpq is the 8th classified SN in a CRG ring and only the 2nd Type Ia (after SN 2025khv)[1].

Akhmetali et al. (2026) — Evolution of fractality in centrally concentrated young clusters

Evolution of fractality in centrally concentrated young clusters

Paper Details

Authors: Akhmetali, A., Assilkhan, A., Mac Low, M.-M., Ussipov, N., Zaidyn, M., Abdikamalov, E., Sills, A., Pang, X., Shukirgaliyev, B.
arXiv: 2603.16183
Submitted: 2026-03-17
Journal: Submitted to A&A
Type: Numerical simulations / MHD + N-body
Simulation code: Torch (FLASH + AMUSE)
Key concepts: Star formation
Molecular cloud
Fractal structure of star clusters
Q parameter (fractality)
Star cluster formation
Stellar feedback
Dynamical relaxation
Subcluster formation
Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • Young star clusters inherit fractal substructure from their parental molecular clouds, as quantified by the Q parameter1.
  • Fractal substructure is erased by dynamical relaxation at approximately 2.5 free-fall times (tff), regardless of simulation resolution or initial degree of fractality2.
  • This ~2.5 tff timescale matches results from Laverde-Villarreal et al. (2025) across clouds an order of magnitude more massive, suggesting a near-universal scaling3.
  • Massive star feedback can trigger secondary subcluster formation, but its effect is stochastic: in 3 of 13 simulations subclusters maintained fractality beyond 4 tff; in others, massive stars formed without enhancing fractality2.
  • The Q parameter correlates positively with both the box-counting and correlation fractal dimensions, with the correlation dimension showing a stronger (moderate) correlation due to its sensitivity to local clustering2.

Implications

The ~2.5 tff timescale for fractal erasure appears to be set by global free-fall dynamics rather than local initial conditions, feedback physics, or resolution, pointing to a fundamental physical clock governing cluster assembly. This matters for galaxy evolution studies: observations of embedded clusters sample a time-evolving structural sequence, and clusters cannot be assumed to be structurally static. Stochastic massive-star feedback introduces scatter into this picture, and in some environments can significantly prolong hierarchical, fractal morphologies — with potential consequences for the initial conditions of star cluster dissolution and the field star population.

Other works

This paper directly extends the Torch simulation suite of Assilkhan et al. (2026)4, adding fractal diagnostics to their centrally concentrated cluster formation runs. The ~2.5 tff result corroborates Laverde-Villarreal et al. (2025)3 but contrasts with Ballone et al. (2020)5, who found clusters remained fractal after 2 tff in much more massive (104–105 M☉) clouds — possibly reflecting different density profiles or physics. Earlier SPH-based work by Schmeja & Klessen (2006)6 and Maschberger et al. (2010)7 established the foundational picture of early-low-Q evolution, but lacked self-consistent stellar feedback. Parker & Dale (2015)8 and Gavagnin et al. (2017)9 found little effect of feedback on Q — a conclusion this paper nuances: feedback can matter, but stochastically.

Summary

Akhmetali et al. (2026) investigate how the fractal spatial structure of young star clusters evolves during and after their formation from centrally concentrated molecular clouds. Using a suite of 13 magnetohydrodynamic N-body simulations run with the Torch framework (FLASH + AMUSE), they track the Q parameter and two fractal dimension estimators — box-counting and correlation dimension — across different simulation resolutions and random seed realizations of a 2513 M☉ cloud42.

The central finding is that fractal substructure, inherited from the parent cloud, is erased on a timescale of ~2.5 tff through dynamical relaxation — a result that is robust across the full range of resolutions and early fractality levels2. In three models where massive stars trigger secondary subcluster formation, fractality persists beyond 4 tff. The paper also provides the first systematic comparison of Q with two different fractal dimension estimators in physically motivated (rather than artificial) cluster simulations.

Key Findings

Fractal inheritance and erasure timescale

All simulated clusters begin with fractal substructure (Q < 0.8 in 2D, Q < 0.7 in 3D), inherited from the collapsing molecular cloud1011. In 10 of 13 simulations, Q exceeds the fractal threshold at approximately 2.5 tff, independent of resolution (refinement levels n3 through n6) and independent of the initial degree of fractality2. Because all simulations share the same global density profile and mean volume density, the authors conclude that the dissolution timescale is primarily regulated by the global free-fall timescale of the parent cloud2. The same timescale was found by Laverde-Villarreal et al. (2025) for clouds 10× more massive3.

Subcluster formation mechanisms

Three models (n3s6, n3s7, n4s1) develop persistent subclusters that maintain Q below the fractal threshold beyond 4 tff. Two formation pathways are identified2:

  • Feedback-triggered: A massive star forms, expels surrounding gas, which sweeps up a dense shell where a second subcluster nucleates (model n3s6).
  • Dynamical: Two subclusters form in close proximity, begin to merge, then separate again through dynamical interactions (models n3s7, n4s1).

Critically, feedback-driven subclustering is not universal: in other simulations with massive stars (e.g., n3s1, n3s8, n5s1), no enhancement of fractality results. The outcome depends sensitively on the mass and formation timing of the massive star2.

Fractal dimension evolution

The box-counting dimension (DBC) and correlation dimension (DCD) both increase steeply during the first free-fall time as the cluster assembles2. For n3s* models, DCD,3D plateaus at ~2.4 while DBC,3D stabilizes at ~1.8–1.9; 2D projections of both methods converge to ~1.5–1.6. In the highest-resolution models (n5s1, n6s1), which develop strongly concentrated clusters (Q ~ 1.2), DCD continues to rise in 3D beyond 2.5 while DBC plateaus at ~1.3–1.4 — reflecting the correlation dimension's higher sensitivity to dense stellar cores versus the box-counting method's global spatial measure2.

Q–fractal dimension correlation

Examining only fractal snapshots (Q < 0.7), the Spearman rank correlation coefficient between Q and DBC is weakly positive, while the correlation with DCD is moderately positive — and both are stable across different minimum-N thresholds2. This contrasts with Sánchez & Alfaro (2009)12, who found strong Q–fdim correlations in artificially constructed fractal clusters. The discrepancy arises because physical simulations produce stellar distributions shaped by gravity, feedback, and initial conditions, not idealized fractal geometry.

Methodology

The simulation suite is adopted from Assilkhan et al. (2026)4. The Torch framework1314 couples the adaptive-mesh FLASH MHD code1516 to the AMUSE N-body environment171819, enabling self-consistent treatment of gas dynamics, star formation via sink particles2021, stellar evolution (SeBa code2223), gravitational N-body integration (ph4 Hermite integrator24), and radiative transfer feedback (photoionization).

Initial conditions follow Shukirgaliyev et al. (2017)25 and Parmentier & Pfalzner (2013)26: gas mass 2513 M☉, sphere radius 5.5 pc, Plummer-like central concentration, T = 20 K, virial parameter αvir = 0.5, vertical magnetic field Bz = 3 × 10−6 G. Refinement level n is varied from 3 to 6 (resolution 643–5123); n=3 runs have 10 random seeds for IMF stochasticity27.

Fractality is quantified via: (1) the Q parameter1 (Q = m̄/s̄; MST mean edge length over mean interparticle separation; computed in both 2D and 3D following Cartwright 200928); (2) box-counting dimension2930 estimated from the slope of ln N(L) vs. −ln L; (3) correlation dimension31 estimated from the correlation integral C(r).

Literature Context

The fractal nature of young stellar clusters has been established observationally1123233 and in simulations67. Clusters range from near-fractal ρ Ophiuchus (concentrated at ~1 Myr) to the substructured NGC 1513 and NGC 1641 (>100 Myr)12. Most prior hydrodynamical work used simpler physics: Schmeja & Klessen (2006)6 used SPH without feedback; Maschberger et al. (2010)7 used pure gravity; Girichidis et al. (2012)34 used small, unstable clouds. This paper introduces the full Torch physics suite.

Evolution of fractality in centrally concentrated young clusters

Paper Details

Authors: Akhmetali, A., Assilkhan, A., Mac Low, M.-M., Ussipov, N., Zaidyn, M., Abdikamalov, E., Sills, A., Pang, X., Shukirgaliyev, B.

Journal: Submitted to A&A
Type: Numerical simulations / MHD + N-body
Simulation code: Torch (FLASH + AMUSE)
Key concepts: Star formation

Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • Young star clusters inherit fractal substructure from their parental molecular clouds, as quantified by the Q parameter1.
  • Fractal substructure is erased by dynamical relaxation at approximately 2.5 free-fall times (tff), regardless of simulation resolution or initial degree of fractality2.
  • This ~2.5 tff timescale matches results from Laverde-Villarreal et al. (2025) across clouds an order of magnitude more massive, suggesting a near-universal scaling3.
  • Massive star feedback can trigger secondary subcluster formation, but its effect is stochastic: in 3 of 13 simulations subclusters maintained fractality beyond 4 tff; in others, massive stars formed without enhancing fractality2.
  • The Q parameter correlates positively with both the box-counting and correlation fractal dimensions, with the correlation dimension showing a stronger (moderate) correlation due to its sensitivity to local clustering2.

Implications

The ~2.5 tff timescale for fractal erasure appears to be set by global free-fall dynamics rather than local initial conditions, feedback physics, or resolution, pointing to a fundamental physical clock governing cluster assembly. This matters for galaxy evolution studies: observations of embedded clusters sample a time-evolving structural sequence, and clusters cannot be assumed to be structurally static. Stochastic massive-star feedback introduces scatter into this picture, and in some environments can significantly prolong hierarchical, fractal morphologies — with potential consequences for the initial conditions of star cluster dissolution and the field star population.

Other works

This paper directly extends the Torch simulation suite of Assilkhan et al. (2026)4, adding fractal diagnostics to their centrally concentrated cluster formation runs. The ~2.5 tff result corroborates Laverde-Villarreal et al. (2025)3 but contrasts with Ballone et al. (2020)5, who found clusters remained fractal after 2 tff in much more massive (104–105 M☉) clouds — possibly reflecting different density profiles or physics. Earlier SPH-based work by Schmeja & Klessen (2006)6 and Maschberger et al. (2010)7 established the foundational picture of early-low-Q evolution, but lacked self-consistent stellar feedback. Parker & Dale (2015)8 and Gavagnin et al. (2017)9 found little effect of feedback on Q — a conclusion this paper nuances: feedback can matter, but stochastically.

Summary

Akhmetali et al. (2026) investigate how the fractal spatial structure of young star clusters evolves during and after their formation from centrally concentrated molecular clouds. Using a suite of 13 magnetohydrodynamic N-body simulations run with the Torch framework (FLASH + AMUSE), they track the Q parameter and two fractal dimension estimators — box-counting and correlation dimension — across different simulation resolutions and random seed realizations of a 2513 M☉ cloud42.

The central finding is that fractal substructure, inherited from the parent cloud, is erased on a timescale of ~2.5 tff through dynamical relaxation — a result that is robust across the full range of resolutions and early fractality levels2. In three models where massive stars trigger secondary subcluster formation, fractality persists beyond 4 tff. The paper also provides the first systematic comparison of Q with two different fractal dimension estimators in physically motivated (rather than artificial) cluster simulations.

Key Findings

Fractal inheritance and erasure timescale

All simulated clusters begin with fractal substructure (Q < 0.8 in 2D, Q < 0.7 in 3D), inherited from the collapsing molecular cloud1011. In 10 of 13 simulations, Q exceeds the fractal threshold at approximately 2.5 tff, independent of resolution (refinement levels n3 through n6) and independent of the initial degree of fractality2. Because all simulations share the same global density profile and mean volume density, the authors conclude that the dissolution timescale is primarily regulated by the global free-fall timescale of the parent cloud2. The same timescale was found by Laverde-Villarreal et al. (2025) for clouds 10× more massive3.

  • Dynamical: Two subclusters form in close proximity, begin to merge, then separate again through dynamical interactions (models n3s7, n4s1).

Critically, feedback-driven subclustering is not universal: in other simulations with massive stars (e.g., n3s1, n3s8, n5s1), no enhancement of fractality results. The outcome depends sensitively on the mass and formation timing of the massive star2.

Examining only fractal snapshots (Q < 0.7), the Spearman rank correlation coefficient between Q and DBC is weakly positive, while the correlation with DCD is moderately positive — and both are stable across different minimum-N thresholds2. This contrasts with Sánchez & Alfaro (2009)12, who found strong Q–fdim correlations in artificially constructed fractal clusters. The discrepancy arises because physical simulations produce stellar distributions shaped by gravity, feedback, and initial conditions, not idealized fractal geometry.

Jadhav et al. (2025) — Hourglass Magnetic Field in IRDC G351.77-0.53

Unveiling an Hourglass-Shaped magnetic field toward IRDC G351.77–0.53

Paper Details

Authors: O. R. Jadhav, L. K. Dewangan, I. I. Zinchenko, T. G. S. Pillai, P. Sanhueza, A. K. Maity, R. K. Yadav, S. Sharma
arXiv: 2603.16425
Submitted: 2025
Type: Observational / Far-infrared dust polarimetry
Instrument: SOFIA/HAWC+ 214 μm
Key concepts: Magnetic fields in star formation
DCF method
Infrared dark clouds
Dust polarimetry
Filamentary star formation
Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • SOFIA/HAWC+ 214 μm polarimetry reveals a clear hourglass B-field morphology centered on the massive clump c1 in IRDC G351.77–0.53, with the large-scale field perpendicular to the filament consistent with Planck 353 GHz observations [1][2].
  • DCF analysis yields a mean plane-of-sky field strength BPOS ~ 147 ± 60 μG across the filament, rising to ~0.8 mG near c1; the mass-to-flux ratio λ ≈ 0.9 ± 0.6 places the cloud at the transcritical boundary [1][3].
  • Magnetic (EB ~ 3.2 × 1047 erg), gravitational (EG ~ 1.9 × 1047 erg), and kinetic (EK ~ 1.8 × 1047 erg) energies are comparable, confirming B-fields are dynamically important in governing collapse and fragmentation [1].
  • The hourglass morphology is consistent with two mechanisms: magnetically regulated gravitational collapse (Nakamura & Li 2008 [4]) and/or converging sub-filaments aligned with B-field lines (Leurini et al. 2019 [5]; Pillai et al. 2020 [6]) [1].

Implications

The near-unity mass-to-flux ratio places G351 c1 at the transcritical boundary — neither magnetically dominated nor freely collapsing — constraining models of how massive filaments fragment and feed high-mass star formation. While this IRDC is not directly relevant to galaxy evolution, the result contributes to the broader understanding of star formation efficiency in dense environments, which ultimately sets the stellar mass function and feedback budget in galaxies. The unresolved pinching-vs-inflow ambiguity highlights a key observational frontier for ALMA-scale polarimetry.

Other Works

G351 shows striking similarity to OMC-1 (Pattle et al. 2017; Chuss et al. 2019) and DR21 (Ching et al. 2022), where B-fields are perpendicular to filaments on large scales but form hourglasses in dense interiors. However, hourglass-shaped B-fields in IRDCs specifically are rare — most IRDC B-field studies (G11: Ngoc+2023; G14: Santos+2016; G28: Law+2024; G34: Tang+2019; G47: Stephens+2022) find ordered fields without clear hourglass signatures. The converging sub-filament interpretation parallels Pillai et al. (2020) in Serpens South and Cox et al. (2016) in Musca.

Summary

This paper presents SOFIA/HAWC+ 214 μm far-infrared dust polarimetric observations of the infrared dark cloud (IRDC) G351.77–0.53, a massive filamentary cloud at ~2.0 kpc hosting the protocluster IRAS 17233–3606 [1]. The observations reveal a striking hourglass-shaped magnetic field morphology centered on the densest clump (c1), where the field lines are pinched inward — a classic signature of magnetically regulated gravitational collapse [1].

Using the Davis-Chandrasekhar-Fermi (DCF) method, the authors estimate a mean plane-of-sky B-field strength of ~147 ± 60 μG across the filament, increasing to ~0.8 mG near the central clump [1]. The mass-to-flux ratio λ ≈ 0.9 ± 0.6 indicates the cloud is magnetically transcritical — poised between magnetic support and gravitational collapse [1]. An energy budget analysis confirms that magnetic, gravitational, and kinetic energies are comparable, ruling out scenarios where any single energy component dominates [1].

Key Findings

Hourglass B-Field Morphology

The large-scale B-field is perpendicular to the filament axis, consistent with Planck 850 μm observations [2], but transitions to an hourglass morphology toward clump c1. This pinching pattern is interpreted as gravitational contraction dragging field lines inward [1]. The hourglass is centered on the column density peak and aligned with the filament minor axis.

Magnetic Field Strength & Criticality

DCF-derived B-field strengths range from ~60 μG in the diffuse envelope to ~0.8 mG at clump c1 [1]. The transcritical mass-to-flux ratio (λ ≈ 0.9) means the cloud sits at the boundary where magnetic support is roughly balanced by self-gravity — consistent with theories predicting that massive clumps reach this state before fragmenting into cores [3][4].

Energy Equipartition

The ratio of magnetic-to-gravitational energy and kinetic-to-gravitational energy are both of order unity across the filament, suggesting a three-way energy balance [1]. This is distinct from either magnetically subcritical clouds (where B dominates) or supercritical clouds (where gravity dominates), and places G351 in a dynamically complex regime.

Pinching vs. Inflow Ambiguity

The hourglass morphology could arise from gravitational contraction pinching an initially uniform field (classical interpretation) or from converging sub-filaments carrying their own field lines into the clump [1]. The current resolution cannot distinguish these scenarios — both produce similar projected morphologies.

Methodology

SOFIA/HAWC+ Band E (214 μm) polarimetry at 18.2″ resolution (~0.18 pc at 2 kpc) [1]. Complementary data includes Herschel column density and temperature maps, ALMA 1.38 mm continuum, and molecular line data (N₂H⁺, HNC, HCO⁺) for kinematic analysis. The DCF method estimates B-field strength from the dispersion of polarization angles (δθ) combined with gas density and velocity dispersion [5][6].

Literature Context

G351 joins a growing sample of IRDCs with resolved B-field morphologies from far-infrared/submm polarimetry: G11.11–0.12 [7], G14.225–0.506 [8], G28.23–0.19 [9], G34.43+0.24 [10], and G47.06+0.26 [11]. Hourglass morphologies have been observed in several of these, suggesting that magnetically regulated collapse is a common feature of massive clumps. The transcritical mass-to-flux ratio is broadly consistent with results from the JCMT/BISTRO survey [12] and the SOFIA/HAWC+ FIELDMAPS program [13].

Unveiling an Hourglass-Shaped magnetic field toward IRDC G351.77–0.53

Paper Details

Authors: O. R. Jadhav, L. K. Dewangan, I. I. Zinchenko, T. G. S. Pillai, P. Sanhueza, A. K. Maity, R. K. Yadav, S. Sharma
arXiv: 2603.16425
Submitted: 2025
Type: Observational / Far-infrared dust polarimetry
Instrument: SOFIA/HAWC+ 214 μm
Key concepts: Magnetic fields in star formation
DCF method
Infrared dark clouds
Dust polarimetry
Filamentary star formation
Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • SOFIA/HAWC+ 214 μm polarimetry reveals a clear hourglass B-field morphology centered on the massive clump c1 in IRDC G351.77–0.53, with the large-scale field perpendicular to the filament consistent with Planck 353 GHz observations [1][2].
  • DCF analysis yields a mean plane-of-sky field strength BPOS ~ 147 ± 60 μG across the filament, rising to ~0.8 mG near c1; the mass-to-flux ratio λ ≈ 0.9 ± 0.6 places the cloud at the transcritical boundary [1][3].
  • Magnetic (EB ~ 3.2 × 1047 erg), gravitational (EG ~ 1.9 × 1047 erg), and kinetic (EK ~ 1.8 × 1047 erg) energies are comparable, confirming B-fields are dynamically important in governing collapse and fragmentation [1].
  • The hourglass morphology is consistent with two mechanisms: magnetically regulated gravitational collapse (Nakamura & Li 2008 [4]) and/or converging sub-filaments aligned with B-field lines (Leurini et al. 2019 [5]; Pillai et al. 2020 [6]) [1].

Implications

The near-unity mass-to-flux ratio places G351 c1 at the transcritical boundary — neither magnetically dominated nor freely collapsing — constraining models of how massive filaments fragment and feed high-mass star formation. While this IRDC is not directly relevant to galaxy evolution, the result contributes to the broader understanding of star formation efficiency in dense environments, which ultimately sets the stellar mass function and feedback budget in galaxies. The unresolved pinching-vs-inflow ambiguity highlights a key observational frontier for ALMA-scale polarimetry.

Other Works

G351 shows striking similarity to OMC-1 (Pattle et al. 2017; Chuss et al. 2019) and DR21 (Ching et al. 2022), where B-fields are perpendicular to filaments on large scales but form hourglasses in dense interiors. However, hourglass-shaped B-fields in IRDCs specifically are rare — most IRDC B-field studies (G11: Ngoc+2023; G14: Santos+2016; G28: Law+2024; G34: Tang+2019; G47: Stephens+2022) find ordered fields without clear hourglass signatures. The converging sub-filament interpretation parallels Pillai et al. (2020) in Serpens South and Cox et al. (2016) in Musca.

Summary

This paper presents SOFIA/HAWC+ 214 μm far-infrared dust polarimetric observations of the infrared dark cloud (IRDC) G351.77–0.53, a massive filamentary cloud at ~2.0 kpc hosting the protocluster IRAS 17233–3606 [1]. The observations reveal a striking hourglass-shaped magnetic field morphology centered on the densest clump (c1), where the field lines are pinched inward — a classic signature of magnetically regulated gravitational collapse [1].

Using the Davis-Chandrasekhar-Fermi (DCF) method, the authors estimate a mean plane-of-sky B-field strength of ~147 ± 60 μG across the filament, increasing to ~0.8 mG near the central clump [1]. The mass-to-flux ratio λ ≈ 0.9 ± 0.6 indicates the cloud is magnetically transcritical — poised between magnetic support and gravitational collapse [1]. An energy budget analysis confirms that magnetic, gravitational, and kinetic energies are comparable, ruling out scenarios where any single energy component dominates [1].

Key Findings

Hourglass B-Field Morphology

The large-scale B-field is perpendicular to the filament axis, consistent with Planck 850 μm observations [2], but transitions to an hourglass morphology toward clump c1. This pinching pattern is interpreted as gravitational contraction dragging field lines inward [1]. The hourglass is centered on the column density peak and aligned with the filament minor axis.

Magnetic Field Strength & Criticality

DCF-derived B-field strengths range from ~60 μG in the diffuse envelope to ~0.8 mG at clump c1 [1]. The transcritical mass-to-flux ratio (λ ≈ 0.9) means the cloud sits at the boundary where magnetic support is roughly balanced by self-gravity — consistent with theories predicting that massive clumps reach this state before fragmenting into cores [3][4].

Energy Equipartition

The ratio of magnetic-to-gravitational energy and kinetic-to-gravitational energy are both of order unity across the filament, suggesting a three-way energy balance [1]. This is distinct from either magnetically subcritical clouds (where B dominates) or supercritical clouds (where gravity dominates), and places G351 in a dynamically complex regime.

Pinching vs. Inflow Ambiguity

The hourglass morphology could arise from gravitational contraction pinching an initially uniform field (classical interpretation) or from converging sub-filaments carrying their own field lines into the clump [1]. The current resolution cannot distinguish these scenarios — both produce similar projected morphologies.

Methodology

SOFIA/HAWC+ Band E (214 μm) polarimetry at 18.2″ resolution (~0.18 pc at 2 kpc) [1]. Complementary data includes Herschel column density and temperature maps, ALMA 1.38 mm continuum, and molecular line data (N₂H⁺, HNC, HCO⁺) for kinematic analysis. The DCF method estimates B-field strength from the dispersion of polarization angles (δθ) combined with gas density and velocity dispersion [5][6].

Literature Context

G351 joins a growing sample of IRDCs with resolved B-field morphologies from far-infrared/submm polarimetry: G11.11–0.12 [7], G14.225–0.506 [8], G28.23–0.19 [9], G34.43+0.24 [10], and G47.06+0.26 [11]. Hourglass morphologies have been observed in several of these, suggesting that magnetically regulated collapse is a common feature of massive clumps. The transcritical mass-to-flux ratio is broadly consistent with results from the JCMT/BISTRO survey [12] and the SOFIA/HAWC+ FIELDMAPS program [13].
