Peretti et al. (2026) — Particle acceleration at recollimation shocks

Particle acceleration at recollimation shocks in sub-relativistic jets

Paper Details

Authors: Peretti, E., Amato, E., Cerri, S. S., Morlino, G., Pullano, L. P., Recchia, S.
arXiv: 2603.16647
Submitted: 2026-03-18
Type: Theoretical / Semi-analytic modeling
Key concepts: Recollimation shocks
Jet-cocoon dynamics
Diffusive shock acceleration
Turbulence & diffusion regimes
Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • Semi-analytic model extends Bromberg et al. (2011) jet-cocoon hydrodynamics to the sub-relativistic regime, deriving cocoon pressure Pc ∝ Lj2/5 ρA3/5 t−4/5 and recollimation shock location as a function of jet luminosity [1].
  • Oblique recollimation shocks retain strong normal-component compression ratios sufficient for efficient DSA, with acceleration efficiency parametrized by shock inclination angle ψ [1].
  • Predicted maximum proton energies span three orders of magnitude by source class: protostellar jets up to TeV (HH80-81 prototype [10][11]), microquasars up to tens of PeV (SS433 [5][6]), and Seyfert jets PeV–EeV under Bohm diffusion (NGC 4151 [2], NGC 1068 [4]) [1].
  • Escaping protons diffusing into the jet cocoon produce hadronic gamma-ray and neutrino signatures, potentially explaining TeV detections by HAWC [7], H.E.S.S. [8], and LHAASO [9] around known microquasars and Seyferts [1].

Implications

This work elevates recollimation shocks from a niche hydrodynamic feature to a potentially dominant particle acceleration site across multiple jet classes. If the predicted PeV–EeV proton energies in Seyfert jets are confirmed, sub-relativistic AGN jets become serious contributors to the extragalactic cosmic-ray budget — reshaping how we interpret stationary bright knots in VLBI maps and broadband jet SEDs. For galaxy evolution, the implied hadronic energy injection into jet cocoons adds a non-thermal feedback channel that could influence circumgalactic gas heating.

Other Works

The analytic framework builds directly on the relativistic jet-cocoon model of Bromberg et al. (2011) and aligns with proposals that microquasars are Galactic PeVatrons. The oblique-shock acceleration picture complements earlier numerical work by Gómez et al. (1995) and Komissarov & Falle (1997) on standing shocks in jets, while competing with internal-shock and magnetic-reconnection models for the dominant nonthermal emission mechanism. Recent HAWC and LHAASO detections around SS433 provide the most immediate observational test bed.

Summary

This paper develops a semi-analytic framework for diffusive shock acceleration (DSA) at recollimation shocks in sub-relativistic jets, extending the Bromberg et al. (2011) analytic jet-cocoon model to the non-relativistic regime [1]. The authors solve a spatially-dependent transport equation to predict particle spectra and maximum energies for three jet classes: Seyfert galaxies, microquasars, and protostellar jets [1].

Key Findings

Maximum Particle Energies

Predicted maximum proton energies depend on source class and diffusion regime [1]:

  • Seyfert galaxies (SEY_0): PeV–EeV under Bohm diffusion; parameters from NGC 4151 [2][3] and NGC 1068 [4]
  • Microquasars (MQ_0): Up to tens of PeV; SS433 as prototype [5][6], consistent with HAWC [7], H.E.S.S. [8], and LHAASO [9] detections
  • Protostellar jets (PS_0): Up to TeV; HH80-81 as prototype [10][11]

E_max scales approximately as ε_B^(1/2) ṁ^(1/2) u₀^(3/2) / θ₀ in the Bohm-limit Hillas estimate [1][12]. Departures from Bohm diffusion modify the velocity scaling [1].

Diffusion Regime Dependence

The turbulence spectrum critically determines E_max [1]:

  • Bohm (δ = 1): Most efficient scattering; highest E_max [1]
  • Kraichnan (δ = 1/2): Benchmark regime [1]
  • Kolmogorov (δ = 1/3): Least efficient; can reduce E_max by orders of magnitude [1]

Diffusion coefficient forms are justified by quasi-linear theory and pitch-angle scattering studies [13][14]. Alternative mechanisms such as mirror diffusion [15][16] motivate exploring multiple scalings [1].

Confinement Limits

Upstream confinement — where the particle diffusion length exceeds the shock height — typically sets E_max [1]. Downstream turbulence decay introduces a competing limit: if turbulence decays rapidly behind the shock, particles escape downstream before reaching maximum energy [1]. The dominant constraint depends on the specific source parameters and diffusion regime [1].

Methodology

Jet-Cocoon Hydrodynamics

The paper extends the Bromberg et al. (2011) analytic jet-cocoon framework [17] to sub-relativistic velocities (u₀ ~ 0.01–0.3c) [1]. This builds on earlier analytic work by Krause (2003) [18] and draws on the wind-blown bubble analogy of Weaver et al. (1977) [19]. The model derives:

  • Cocoon pressure P_c(t) ∝ t^(-4/5) [1][17][19]
  • Jet-head position z_h(t) ∝ t^(3/5) [1][17]
  • Recollimation-shock location ẑ(t) and oblique compression ratio via k_p parameter [1]

Early hydrodynamic simulations by Norman et al. (1982) first identified cocoons and shocked environments [20]. MHD simulations by Mizuno et al. (2015) [21], Moll et al. (2008) [22], and Bodo et al. (2018) [23] inform the role of magnetic field geometry, kink instabilities, and radiative cooling on recollimation shock properties. Giannios (2006) justified the hydrodynamic approximation by showing Poynting flux dissipates rapidly, leaving jets kinetically dominated at large scales [24].

Transport Equation

A semi-analytic 1D spatially-dependent transport equation describes particle injection at the shock, upstream diffusion, and downstream advection [1]. The methodology follows precedents set by Morlino et al. (2021) for wind termination shocks [25] and prior applications by Peretti et al. (2022, 2023) and Mukhopadhyay (2023) in related systems [26][27][28].

An iterative algorithm couples the transport solution to the shock geometry, yielding a shock spectrum f_sh(p) with cutoff functions that encode the confinement physics [1].

Literature Context

Jet Launching and Shock Theory

The paper situates itself within a rich history of jet physics. Energy extraction from compact objects was first proposed by Penrose (1969) [29]. The Blandford-Znajek mechanism [30] and magneto-centrifugal disk winds [31] provide the standard jet-launching frameworks, supported by early MHD simulations [32] and modern GRMHD work showing magnetically arrested disks produce powerful jets [33].

The connection between shocks and jet emission was established by Blandford & Rees (1974) [34] and Scheuer (1974) [35]. Shock propagation and standing-shock patterns in jets were studied extensively by Marscher & Gear (1985) [36], Daly & Marscher (1988) [37], Gómez et al. (1995, 1997, 2000) [38][39][40], and Komissarov & Falle (1997) [41].

Observational Motivation

Microquasars: Discovery of superluminal motion by Mirabel & Rodríguez (1994, 1998) [42][43] established microquasars as relativistic jet sources. Extensive modeling of microquasar cosmic-ray and gamma-ray production followed [44][45][46][47][48][49][50][51]. Recent high-energy detections — HAWC at SS433 [7], H.E.S.S. at SS433 [8], LHAASO Galactic sources [9] — along with studies by Abaroa et al. (2024) [52] and Bykov et al. (2025) [53] reinforce microquasars as PeVatron candidates.

Seyfert galaxies: Ulvestad & Wilson (2005) [2], Williams & Bower (2017) [3], and Salvatore et al. (2023) [4] provide the observational basis for Seyfert jet parameters.

Protostellar jets: Rodríguez-Kamenetzky et al. (2017) [10] and Carrasco-González et al. (2021) [11] detected synchrotron emission from protostellar jets. Padovani et al. (2015) [54], Araudo et al. (2021) [55], and de Oña Wilhelmi et al. (2023) [56] modeled non-thermal processes in these systems.

What This Paper Supports

  • Bromberg-style analytic jet-cocoon modeling as a useful framework in the sub-relativistic regime [17]
  • Observational interpretations that microquasars and Seyfert jets can accelerate particles to PeV energies, consistent with HAWC/H.E.S.S./LHAASO detections [7][8][9]
  • Wind-bubble analogies for deriving self-similar cocoon scalings [19][25]

What This Paper Nuances or Challenges

  • The paper highlights that not all jet shocks are efficient PeV accelerators: Kolmogorov-like diffusion can severely limit E_max in low-power/low-velocity jets, contrasting optimistic universal Bohm assumptions in some prior phenomenology [1]
  • Relativistic-shock DSA inefficiency motivates focusing on sub-relativistic jets as acceleration sites — a shift from the common emphasis on relativistic shocks [1]
  • Oblique-shock effects remain incompletely captured: the 1D model omits guide-field physics studied by Kirk & Heavens (1989) [57], Gieseler et al. (1999) [58], Bell (2011) [59], Kirk et al. (2023) [60], and Reville et al. (2025) [61]

Model Limitations

  • 1D treatment: Neglects lateral transport and multi-dimensional effects [1]
  • Simplified obliquity: Guide-field effects not fully captured [1][57][59]
  • Quasi-stationary assumption: Does not model jet variability or time-dependent shocks [1]
  • Sub-relativistic only: Relativistic generalization needed for fast AGN/blazar jets; Perucho et al. (2007, 2014, 2022) provide relevant simulations [62][63][64]
  • Cocoon microphysics: Jet-head details and particle escape into the cocoon are treated simply [1]

  • Oblique recollimation shocks retain strong normal-component compression ratios sufficient for efficient DSA, with acceleration efficiency parametrized by shock inclination angle ψ [1].
  • Predicted maximum proton energies span three orders of magnitude by source class: protostellar jets up to TeV (HH80-81 prototype [10][11]), microquasars up to tens of PeV (SS433 [5][6]), and Seyfert jets PeV–EeV under Bohm diffusion (NGC 4151 [2], NGC 1068 [4]) [1].
  • Escaping protons diffusing into the jet cocoon produce hadronic gamma-ray and neutrino signatures, potentially explaining TeV detections by HAWC [7], H.E.S.S. [8], and LHAASO [9] around known microquasars and Seyferts [1].

Implications

This work elevates recollimation shocks from a niche hydrodynamic feature to a potentially dominant particle acceleration site across multiple jet classes. If the predicted PeV–EeV proton energies in Seyfert jets are confirmed, sub-relativistic AGN jets become serious contributors to the extragalactic cosmic-ray budget — reshaping how we interpret stationary bright knots in VLBI maps and broadband jet SEDs. For galaxy evolution, the implied hadronic energy injection into jet cocoons adds a non-thermal feedback channel that could influence circumgalactic gas heating.

Other Works

The analytic framework builds directly on the relativistic jet-cocoon model of Bromberg et al. (2011) and aligns with proposals that microquasars are Galactic PeVatrons. The oblique-shock acceleration picture complements earlier numerical work by Gómez et al. (1995) and Komissarov & Falle (1997) on standing shocks in jets, while competing with internal-shock and magnetic-reconnection models for the dominant nonthermal emission mechanism. Recent HAWC and LHAASO detections around SS433 provide the most immediate observational test bed.

Summary

This paper develops a semi-analytic framework for diffusive shock acceleration (DSA) at recollimation shocks in sub-relativistic jets, extending the Bromberg et al. (2011) analytic jet-cocoon model to the non-relativistic regime [1]. The authors solve a spatially-dependent transport equation to predict particle spectra and maximum energies for three jet classes: Seyfert galaxies, microquasars, and protostellar jets [1].

Key Findings

Maximum Particle Energies

Predicted maximum proton energies depend on source class and diffusion regime [1]:

  • Seyfert galaxies (SEY_0): PeV–EeV under Bohm diffusion; parameters from NGC 4151 [2][3] and NGC 1068 [4]
  • Microquasars (MQ_0): Up to tens of PeV; SS433 as prototype [5][6], consistent with HAWC [7], H.E.S.S. [8], and LHAASO [9] detections
  • Protostellar jets (PS_0): Up to TeV; HH80-81 as prototype [10][11]

E_max scales approximately as ε_B^(1/2) ṁ^(1/2) u₀^(3/2) / θ₀ in the Bohm-limit Hillas estimate [1][12]. Departures from Bohm diffusion modify the velocity scaling [1].

Diffusion Regime Dependence

The turbulence spectrum critically determines E_max [1]:

  • Bohm (δ = 1): Most efficient scattering; highest E_max [1]
  • Kraichnan (δ = 1/2): Benchmark regime [1]
  • Kolmogorov (δ = 1/3): Least efficient; can reduce E_max by orders of magnitude [1]

Diffusion coefficient forms are justified by quasi-linear theory and pitch-angle scattering studies [13][14]. Alternative mechanisms such as mirror diffusion [15][16] motivate exploring multiple scalings [1].

Confinement Limits

Upstream confinement — where the particle diffusion length exceeds the shock height — typically sets E_max [1]. Downstream turbulence decay introduces a competing limit: if turbulence decays rapidly behind the shock, particles escape downstream before reaching maximum energy [1]. The dominant constraint depends on the specific source parameters and diffusion regime [1].

Methodology

Jet-Cocoon Hydrodynamics

The paper extends the Bromberg et al. (2011) analytic jet-cocoon framework [17] to sub-relativistic velocities (u₀ ~ 0.01–0.3c) [1]. This builds on earlier analytic work by Krause (2003) [18] and draws on the wind-blown bubble analogy of Weaver et al. (1977) [19]. The model derives:

  • Cocoon pressure P_c(t) ∝ t^(-4/5) [1][17][19]
  • Jet-head position z_h(t) ∝ t^(3/5) [1][17]
  • Recollimation-shock location ẑ(t) and oblique compression ratio via k_p parameter [1]

Early hydrodynamic simulations by Norman et al. (1982) first identified cocoons and shocked environments [20]. MHD simulations by Mizuno et al. (2015) [21], Moll et al. (2008) [22], and Bodo et al. (2018) [23] inform the role of magnetic field geometry, kink instabilities, and radiative cooling on recollimation shock properties. Giannios (2006) justified the hydrodynamic approximation by showing Poynting flux dissipates rapidly, leaving jets kinetically dominated at large scales [24].

Transport Equation

A semi-analytic 1D spatially-dependent transport equation describes particle injection at the shock, upstream diffusion, and downstream advection [1]. The methodology follows precedents set by Morlino et al. (2021) for wind termination shocks [25] and prior applications by Peretti et al. (2022, 2023) and Mukhopadhyay (2023) in related systems [26][27][28].

An iterative algorithm couples the transport solution to the shock geometry, yielding a shock spectrum f_sh(p) with cutoff functions that encode the confinement physics [1].

Literature Context

Jet Launching and Shock Theory

The paper situates itself within a rich history of jet physics. Energy extraction from compact objects was first proposed by Penrose (1969) [29]. The Blandford-Znajek mechanism [30] and magneto-centrifugal disk winds [31] provide the standard jet-launching frameworks, supported by early MHD simulations [32] and modern GRMHD work showing magnetically arrested disks produce powerful jets [33].

The connection between shocks and jet emission was established by Blandford & Rees (1974) [34] and Scheuer (1974) [35]. Shock propagation and standing-shock patterns in jets were studied extensively by Marscher & Gear (1985) [36], Daly & Marscher (1988) [37], Gómez et al. (1995, 1997, 2000) [38][39][40], and Komissarov & Falle (1997) [41].

Observational Motivation

Microquasars: Discovery of superluminal motion by Mirabel & Rodríguez (1994, 1998) [42][43] established microquasars as relativistic jet sources. Extensive modeling of microquasar cosmic-ray and gamma-ray production followed [44][45][46][47][48][49][50][51]. Recent high-energy detections — HAWC at SS433 [7], H.E.S.S. at SS433 [8], LHAASO Galactic sources [9] — along with studies by Abaroa et al. (2024) [52] and Bykov et al. (2025) [53] reinforce microquasars as PeVatron candidates.

Seyfert galaxies: Ulvestad & Wilson (2005) [2], Williams & Bower (2017) [3], and Salvatore et al. (2023) [4] provide the observational basis for Seyfert jet parameters.

Protostellar jets: Rodríguez-Kamenetzky et al. (2017) [10] and Carrasco-González et al. (2021) [11] detected synchrotron emission from protostellar jets. Padovani et al. (2015) [54], Araudo et al. (2021) [55], and de Oña Wilhelmi et al. (2023) [56] modeled non-thermal processes in these systems.

What This Paper Supports

  • Bromberg-style analytic jet-cocoon modeling as a useful framework in the sub-relativistic regime [17]
  • Observational interpretations that microquasars and Seyfert jets can accelerate particles to PeV energies, consistent with HAWC/H.E.S.S./LHAASO detections [7][8][9]
  • Wind-bubble analogies for deriving self-similar cocoon scalings [19][25]

What This Paper Nuances or Challenges

  • The paper highlights that not all jet shocks are efficient PeV accelerators: Kolmogorov-like diffusion can severely limit E_max in low-power/low-velocity jets, contrasting optimistic universal Bohm assumptions in some prior phenomenology [1]
  • Relativistic-shock DSA inefficiency motivates focusing on sub-relativistic jets as acceleration sites — a shift from the common emphasis on relativistic shocks [1]
  • Oblique-shock effects remain incompletely captured: the 1D model omits guide-field physics studied by Kirk & Heavens (1989) [57], Gieseler et al. (1999) [58], Bell (2011) [59], Kirk et al. (2023) [60], and Reville et al. (2025) [61]

Model Limitations

  • 1D treatment: Neglects lateral transport and multi-dimensional effects [1]
  • Simplified obliquity: Guide-field effects not fully captured [1][57][59]
  • Quasi-stationary assumption: Does not model jet variability or time-dependent shocks [1]
  • Sub-relativistic only: Relativistic generalization needed for fast AGN/blazar jets; Perucho et al. (2007, 2014, 2022) provide relevant simulations [62][63][64]
  • Cocoon microphysics: Jet-head details and particle escape into the cocoon are treated simply [1]

Pedratti et al. (2026) — Coma Cluster Kinematics with DESI

Novel insights on the Coma Cluster kinematics with DESI. I. Linking mass profile, orbital anisotropy and galaxy populations

Paper Details

Authors: Pedratti, S., Pizzuti, L., Fossati, M., Biviano, A., Boselli, A., Ragagnin, A., Carlin, A.
arXiv: 2603.16706
Submitted: 2026-03-18
Journal: Astronomy & Astrophysics (submitted)
Type: Observational / Kinematic analysis
Key concepts: Coma Cluster
Orbital anisotropy
Ram-pressure stripping
NFW profile
Jeans equation
MG-MAMPOSSt
DESI
Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • Jeans-equation analysis via MG-MAMPOSSt of 1651 DESI DR1 member galaxies yields M200 = 1.08+0.08−0.09 (stat) ± 0.09 (syst) × 1015 M☉ with r200 = 2.12 ± 0.06 Mpc for the Coma Cluster [1][3].
  • Blue cloud galaxies show strongly non-Gaussian velocity distributions (A² ≈ 1.56) and less concentrated spatial profiles (rν(BC) ~ 4 rν(RS)), consistent with an infalling population on radial orbits [1][11].
  • Red sequence galaxies are near-Gaussian (A² ≈ 0.29) and virialised, while green valley galaxies are intermediate (A² ≈ 1.27), supporting a BC → GV → RS evolutionary sequence driven by ram-pressure stripping [1][12].
  • Baryon fraction fb = 0.15 ± 0.02 at r200 is consistent with the cosmological Ωb/Ωm [1][5].
  • The kinematic mass sits above weak lensing (Gavazzi+2009 [6]; Cha+2025 [7]) and X-ray+SZ estimates (Mirakhor+2020 [8]), but below Bayesian ML (Ho+2022 [9]) and caustic methods (Benisty+2025 [10]), with 2σ overlap with most [1].

Implications

The population-dependent orbital anisotropy directly connects cluster dynamics to galaxy evolution: blue star-forming galaxies plunge through the cluster core on radial orbits, experiencing efficient ram-pressure stripping that transforms them through the green valley onto the red sequence. This provides kinematic evidence for the environmental quenching pathway in the nearest massive cluster, calibrating the timescale and orbital geometry of the transformation. The precise mass measurement also benchmarks cluster mass estimation methods, revealing systematic tensions between kinematic, lensing, and X-ray approaches.

Other Works

The orbital anisotropy trends align with Mamon et al. (2019) who found more radial blue-galaxy orbits in stacked WINGS clusters, and with Biviano et al. (2025) who measured similar profiles in nine CLASH-VLT clusters. The Coma mass is consistent with the classic Łokas & Mamon (2003) kinematic estimate (1.2 × 1015 M☉) but sits ~2σ above recent weak-lensing values. Pizzuti et al. (2025) found a comparable anisotropy pattern in PSZ2 G067.17+67.46. The ram-pressure stripping interpretation follows the framework of Gunn & Gott (1972) and the observational synthesis of Boselli, Fossati & Sun (2022).

Summary

This paper presents a kinematic analysis of the Coma Cluster (Abell 1656, z = 0.023) using 1651 member galaxies from the DESI DR1 spectroscopic survey [1][2]. The authors apply the Jeans equation via the MG-MAMPOSSt code to jointly reconstruct the total mass profile and orbital anisotropy profile [1][3][4]. They find a best-fit virial mass M200 = 1.08+0.08−0.09 (stat) ± 0.09 (syst) × 1015 M☉ with r200 = 2.12 ± 0.06 Mpc [1]. Splitting galaxies by colour into red sequence, green valley, and blue cloud populations reveals population-dependent orbital anisotropy consistent with ram-pressure stripping along radial orbits [1].

Key Findings

Mass Profile

The preferred mass model is NFW with a generalised Osipkov-Merritt (gOM) anisotropy profile [1]. Key parameters:

  • M200: 1.08+0.08−0.09 (stat) ± 0.09 (syst) × 1015 M☉ [1]
  • r200: 2.12 ± 0.06 (stat) ± 0.07 (syst) Mpc [1]
  • Scale radius rs: 0.48+0.27−0.13 Mpc [1]
  • Dark matter mass M200(DM): 8.6+1.2−0.8 × 1014 M☉ [1]
  • Baryon fraction fb: 0.15 ± 0.02, consistent with Ωb/Ωm [1][5]

This mass sits above weak lensing estimates (5.1 × 1014 M☉ [6]; 7.4 × 1014 M☉ [7]) and X-ray+SZ estimates (8.5 × 1014 M☉ [8]), but below Bayesian deep learning (1.8 × 1015 M☉ [9]) and caustic/virial methods (1.1–2.9 × 1015 M☉ [10]), with 2σ overlap with most estimates [1].

Population-Dependent Orbital Anisotropy

The paper's key novel contribution is the measurement of anisotropy profiles for different galaxy populations [1]:

  • Red sequence (RS): Anderson-Darling A² ≈ 0.29 — near-Gaussian velocity distribution, consistent with a virialised population [1]
  • Blue cloud (BC): A² ≈ 1.56 — strongly non-Gaussian, indicating an infalling population on radial orbits [1]
  • Green valley (GV): A² ≈ 1.27 — intermediate non-Gaussianity, transitional between RS and BC [1]

BC galaxies have a much less concentrated spatial distribution than RS galaxies, with rν(BC) ~ 4 rν(RS) [1][11]. The radial orbital preference of BC galaxies supports the picture of infalling star-forming galaxies being quenched by ram-pressure stripping as they plunge through the cluster core [1][12][13][14].

Coma Cluster Substructure Context

Although Coma was long considered a prototype relaxed cluster, extensive substructure is now recognized [15][16]. The NGC 4839 group, identified in X-ray and galaxy distributions [17][18], is understood to be on secondary infall after its first orbital apocentre [19][20][21]. A recent or ongoing merger between the main cluster and a group associated with NGC 4889/NGC 4874 is also identified [22]. These complex interactions may explain the post-starburst galaxies observed in the Coma periphery [23][24].

Methodology

Data

The sample is drawn from DESI DR1 with >2000 spectra in a single pointing toward Coma, achieving 91% spectroscopic completeness [2]. The cluster centre is defined at NGC 4874 (RA=194.90°, DEC=27.96°) [25]. Interlopers are removed using the Clean algorithm of Mamon et al. (2013) [4]. Stellar masses are computed using CIGALE SPS code with Chabrier IMF [26][27]. Galaxy colour classification follows the (g−r) vs stellar mass scheme of Boselli et al. (2014) [28].

MG-MAMPOSSt Analysis

MG-MAMPOSSt applies the Jeans equation to projected phase-space data via maximum likelihood [3][4]. The method assumes spherical symmetry and a 3D Gaussian velocity distribution, which remains valid even when the LoS distribution departs from Gaussianity [4]. Mass models tested include NFW, generalised NFW (gNFW) [29], and Burkert [30] profiles. Anisotropy models include Tiret [31] and Osipkov-Merritt [32][33] parameterisations. MCMC convergence verified with Gelman-Rubin R̂ < 1.1 [34] using 110,000-step chains with 10,000 burn-in [35][36].

Robustness

Results are tested against alternative centres (X-ray peak at RA=194.94°, DEC=27.95° [37]), different radial cuts, and multi-component mass models separating dark matter from baryons [1].

Literature Context

The Coma Cluster has been central to dark matter studies since Zwicky's (1933) pioneering mass estimate [38]. Kinematic mass estimates from elliptical galaxies gave M200 = 1.2 × 1015 M☉ with ~30% accuracy [39]. The diversity of mass estimation methods applied to Coma — kinematics, weak lensing, X-ray, SZ, machine learning — makes it a benchmark for inter-method comparison [6][7][8][9][10].

The finding that anisotropy profiles are population-dependent aligns with previous work: Mamon et al. (2019) found more radial behaviour for blue galaxies in stacked WINGS clusters [40], while Biviano et al. (2025) found similar trends in CLASH-VLT clusters [36]. However, Hwang & Lee (2008) reported no significant differences between passive and star-forming galaxy orbits [41], suggesting sample size or cluster-specific effects may matter.

Novel insights on the Coma Cluster kinematics with DESI. I. Linking mass profile, orbital anisotropy and galaxy populations

Paper Details

Authors: Pedratti, S., Pizzuti, L., Fossati, M., Biviano, A., Boselli, A., Ragagnin, A., Carlin, A.
arXiv: 2603.16706
Submitted: 2026-03-18
Journal: Astronomy & Astrophysics (submitted)
Type: Observational / Kinematic analysis
Key concepts: Coma Cluster
Orbital anisotropy
Ram-pressure stripping
NFW profile
Jeans equation
MG-MAMPOSSt
DESI
Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • Jeans-equation analysis via MG-MAMPOSSt of 1651 DESI DR1 member galaxies yields M200 = 1.08+0.08−0.09 (stat) ± 0.09 (syst) × 1015 M☉ with r200 = 2.12 ± 0.06 Mpc for the Coma Cluster [1][3].
  • Blue cloud galaxies show strongly non-Gaussian velocity distributions (A² ≈ 1.56) and less concentrated spatial profiles (rν(BC) ~ 4 rν(RS)), consistent with an infalling population on radial orbits [1][11].
  • Red sequence galaxies are near-Gaussian (A² ≈ 0.29) and virialised, while green valley galaxies are intermediate (A² ≈ 1.27), supporting a BC → GV → RS evolutionary sequence driven by ram-pressure stripping [1][12].
  • Baryon fraction fb = 0.15 ± 0.02 at r200 is consistent with the cosmological Ωb/Ωm [1][5].
  • The kinematic mass sits above weak lensing (Gavazzi+2009 [6]; Cha+2025 [7]) and X-ray+SZ estimates (Mirakhor+2020 [8]), but below Bayesian ML (Ho+2022 [9]) and caustic methods (Benisty+2025 [10]), with 2σ overlap with most [1].

Implications

The population-dependent orbital anisotropy directly connects cluster dynamics to galaxy evolution: blue star-forming galaxies plunge through the cluster core on radial orbits, experiencing efficient ram-pressure stripping that transforms them through the green valley onto the red sequence. This provides kinematic evidence for the environmental quenching pathway in the nearest massive cluster, calibrating the timescale and orbital geometry of the transformation. The precise mass measurement also benchmarks cluster mass estimation methods, revealing systematic tensions between kinematic, lensing, and X-ray approaches.

Other Works

The orbital anisotropy trends align with Mamon et al. (2019) who found more radial blue-galaxy orbits in stacked WINGS clusters, and with Biviano et al. (2025) who measured similar profiles in nine CLASH-VLT clusters. The Coma mass is consistent with the classic Łokas & Mamon (2003) kinematic estimate (1.2 × 1015 M☉) but sits ~2σ above recent weak-lensing values. Pizzuti et al. (2025) found a comparable anisotropy pattern in PSZ2 G067.17+67.46. The ram-pressure stripping interpretation follows the framework of Gunn & Gott (1972) and the observational synthesis of Boselli, Fossati & Sun (2022).

Summary

This paper presents a kinematic analysis of the Coma Cluster (Abell 1656, z = 0.023) using 1651 member galaxies from the DESI DR1 spectroscopic survey [1][2]. The authors apply the Jeans equation via the MG-MAMPOSSt code to jointly reconstruct the total mass profile and orbital anisotropy profile [1][3][4]. They find a best-fit virial mass M200 = 1.08+0.08−0.09 (stat) ± 0.09 (syst) × 1015 M☉ with r200 = 2.12 ± 0.06 Mpc [1]. Splitting galaxies by colour into red sequence, green valley, and blue cloud populations reveals population-dependent orbital anisotropy consistent with ram-pressure stripping along radial orbits [1].

Key Findings

Mass Profile

The preferred mass model is NFW with a generalised Osipkov-Merritt (gOM) anisotropy profile [1]. Key parameters:

  • M200: 1.08+0.08−0.09 (stat) ± 0.09 (syst) × 1015 M☉ [1]
  • r200: 2.12 ± 0.06 (stat) ± 0.07 (syst) Mpc [1]
  • Scale radius rs: 0.48+0.27−0.13 Mpc [1]
  • Dark matter mass M200(DM): 8.6+1.2−0.8 × 1014 M☉ [1]
  • Baryon fraction fb: 0.15 ± 0.02, consistent with Ωb/Ωm [1][5]

This mass sits above weak lensing estimates (5.1 × 1014 M☉ [6]; 7.4 × 1014 M☉ [7]) and X-ray+SZ estimates (8.5 × 1014 M☉ [8]), but below Bayesian deep learning (1.8 × 1015 M☉ [9]) and caustic/virial methods (1.1–2.9 × 1015 M☉ [10]), with 2σ overlap with most estimates [1].

Population-Dependent Orbital Anisotropy

The paper's key novel contribution is the measurement of anisotropy profiles for different galaxy populations [1]:

  • Red sequence (RS): Anderson-Darling A² ≈ 0.29 — near-Gaussian velocity distribution, consistent with a virialised population [1]
  • Blue cloud (BC): A² ≈ 1.56 — strongly non-Gaussian, indicating an infalling population on radial orbits [1]
  • Green valley (GV): A² ≈ 1.27 — intermediate non-Gaussianity, transitional between RS and BC [1]

BC galaxies have a much less concentrated spatial distribution than RS galaxies, with rν(BC) ~ 4 rν(RS) [1][11]. The radial orbital preference of BC galaxies supports the picture of infalling star-forming galaxies being quenched by ram-pressure stripping as they plunge through the cluster core [1][12][13][14].

Coma Cluster Substructure Context

Although Coma was long considered a prototype relaxed cluster, extensive substructure is now recognized [15][16]. The NGC 4839 group, identified in X-ray and galaxy distributions [17][18], is understood to be on secondary infall after its first orbital apocentre [19][20][21]. A recent or ongoing merger between the main cluster and a group associated with NGC 4889/NGC 4874 is also identified [22]. These complex interactions may explain the post-starburst galaxies observed in the Coma periphery [23][24].

Methodology

Data

The sample is drawn from DESI DR1 with >2000 spectra in a single pointing toward Coma, achieving 91% spectroscopic completeness [2]. The cluster centre is defined at NGC 4874 (RA=194.90°, DEC=27.96°) [25]. Interlopers are removed using the Clean algorithm of Mamon et al. (2013) [4]. Stellar masses are computed using CIGALE SPS code with Chabrier IMF [26][27]. Galaxy colour classification follows the (g−r) vs stellar mass scheme of Boselli et al. (2014) [28].

MG-MAMPOSSt Analysis

MG-MAMPOSSt applies the Jeans equation to projected phase-space data via maximum likelihood [3][4]. The method assumes spherical symmetry and a 3D Gaussian velocity distribution, which remains valid even when the LoS distribution departs from Gaussianity [4]. Mass models tested include NFW, generalised NFW (gNFW) [29], and Burkert [30] profiles. Anisotropy models include Tiret [31] and Osipkov-Merritt [32][33] parameterisations. MCMC convergence verified with Gelman-Rubin R̂ < 1.1 [34] using 110,000-step chains with 10,000 burn-in [35][36].

Robustness

Results are tested against alternative centres (X-ray peak at RA=194.94°, DEC=27.95° [37]), different radial cuts, and multi-component mass models separating dark matter from baryons [1].

Literature Context

The Coma Cluster has been central to dark matter studies since Zwicky's (1933) pioneering mass estimate [38]. Kinematic mass estimates from elliptical galaxies gave M200 = 1.2 × 1015 M☉ with ~30% accuracy [39]. The diversity of mass estimation methods applied to Coma — kinematics, weak lensing, X-ray, SZ, machine learning — makes it a benchmark for inter-method comparison [6][7][8][9][10].

The finding that anisotropy profiles are population-dependent aligns with previous work: Mamon et al. (2019) found more radial behaviour for blue galaxies in stacked WINGS clusters [40], while Biviano et al. (2025) found similar trends in CLASH-VLT clusters [36]. However, Hwang & Lee (2008) reported no significant differences between passive and star-forming galaxy orbits [41], suggesting sample size or cluster-specific effects may matter.

Park et al. (2026) — Faraday Rotation Measure of 3C 84

Faraday Rotation Measure of 3C 84 and the Circumnuclear Environment of NGC 1275

Paper Details

Authors: Park, J., Kam, M., Yi, K., Cho, I., Lee, S.-S., Byun, D.-Y., Hodgson, J. A., et al.
arXiv: 2603.16796
Submitted: 2026-03-21
Type: Observational / Multi-frequency polarimetric VLBI
Key concepts: Faraday rotation
Bondi accretion
3C 84 / NGC 1275
RIAF/ADAF models
Parsec-scale AGN jets
Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • All RM values in 3C 84 from 1–15 pc follow a single power law RM ∝ r−2.7±0.2 (Pearson r = −0.876, p = 2×10−9), unifying two decades of apparently discrepant measurements from KVN, VLBA, and HSA [1].
  • The Faraday screen is the circumnuclear ambient medium filling the Bondi sphere (r < 11.8 pc), ruling out a RIAF [15], jet boundary layer [16], and localized gas cloud [17] via spatial extent, sign stability, and cold-gas coexistence arguments [1].
  • First spatially resolved ambient B-field measurement in an elliptical galaxy: B ∝ r−1.7 to −2.2 within the Bondi radius, from ~0.1–1.5 μG at 12 pc to mG–G scales near the core, given ne ∝ r−1.5 to −2 [1].
  • The RM power law is much steeper than M87 (k ~ 1 [6]), suggesting fundamentally different circumnuclear environments between these two nearby radio galaxies [1].

Implications

This result challenges the default assumption that large RMs in low-luminosity AGN trace RIAFs or accretion disks — the ambient circumnuclear medium can dominate Faraday rotation. The spatially resolved B-field profile provides a direct observational anchor for MHD models of AGN fueling and feedback in cool-core clusters like Perseus. For galaxy evolution, understanding the magnetic environment at parsec scales constrains how AGN jets interact with and heat the surrounding medium, a critical ingredient in the feedback cycle that regulates star formation in massive ellipticals.

Other Works

Earlier RM studies of 3C 84 by Plambeck et al. (2014) with CARMA/SMA and Nagai et al. (2017) with VLBA measured individual components but could not identify a unified trend. The RIAF interpretation favored by Plambeck et al. and the jet boundary layer model of Kim et al. (2019) are both superseded by this ambient-medium picture. The steep RM ∝ r−2.7 contrasts with M87's shallow profile (Zavala & Taylor 2002), pointing to a diversity in circumnuclear magnetization among low-luminosity AGN. Trippe et al. (2012) first suggested the ambient medium as Faraday screen origin — this work provides the definitive confirmation with spatially resolved data.

Summary

This paper presents multi-frequency polarimetric VLBI observations of 3C 84 (the radio source in NGC 1275) spanning 8–141 GHz from 2015–2024, using the Korean VLBI Network (KVN), Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA), and High Sensitivity Array (HSA) [1]. The authors detect linear polarization from multiple inner jet components spanning 1–15 pc from the black hole and show that all rotation measure (RM) values follow a single power-law RM ∝ r−2.7±0.2 [1]. This unified relation is naturally explained by a common circumnuclear ambient medium within the Bondi radius (~11.8 pc) of NGC 1275, providing the first spatially resolved measurement of the ambient magnetic field in an elliptical galaxy [1].

Key Findings

Unified RM–Distance Power Law

All RM measurements of 3C 84 from 1–15 pc — spanning inner jet components C4a, C4, C3, K, and the southern lobe — follow a single power-law RM ∝ r−2.7±0.2 with Pearson r = −0.876, p = 2.1×10−9 [1]. This unifies RM measurements that had previously seemed discrepant across multiple instruments and two decades of observations [2][3][4][5]. The power-law is much steeper than the RM profile of M87 (k~1) [6].

Circumnuclear Ambient Medium as Faraday Screen

The paper establishes that the Faraday screen is the circumnuclear ambient medium filling the Bondi sphere (r < 11.8 pc) [1]. Three lines of evidence disfavor competing hypotheses:

  • RIAF disfavored: The coexistence of BLR emission lines and FFA disk absorption requires cold gas (~104 K) that would be destroyed if a RIAF extended to parsec scales [1][7][8]. The Eddington ratio (~0.04–0.09%) places 3C 84 near the accretion state transition [1][9].
  • Jet boundary layer disfavored: Consistently positive RM sign (no temporal sign reversals) contrasts with behavior expected for dynamical jet sheaths [1][5]. RM values > 105 rad/m2 are unusual for boundary layers in nearby AGN [1].
  • Gas cloud disfavored: Persistent RM after hotspot disruption and spatially extended RM across multiple components rule out a localized origin [1][4][10].

First Spatially Resolved Ambient B-Field in an Elliptical Galaxy

By combining the RM profile with electron density models (ne ∝ r−1.5 to −2), the paper constrains B ∝ r−1.7 to −2.2 within the Bondi radius [1]. At the Bondi radius (11.8 pc), B = 0.1–1.5 μG, increasing rapidly inward toward mG–G near the VLBI core [1]. Previous elliptical galaxy magnetic field measurements were galaxy-averaged or theoretical [11][12][13].

New Polarization Detections

First polarization detections from jet components C4 (emerging robustly in 2022 December at RM = 2.6–3.5×105 rad/m2) and K (RM = 0.5–0.9×105 rad/m2) [1]. The consistent ordering C4 > C3 > K within the same epochs establishes a clear distance-dependent RM gradient [1]. HSA 8 GHz confirmed the southern lobe RM (~7000 rad/m2) is unchanged from the Taylor (2006) measurement 20 years prior [2].

Methodology

Multi-Frequency Polarimetric VLBI

The KVN's simultaneous multi-frequency capability (43–141 GHz) is uniquely suited for large RM measurements because narrow frequency spacing resolves the nπ ambiguity [1][14]. An upgraded 16-Gbps OCTAD recording system (post-2022) enabled simultaneous 4-band observations [1]. D-term calibration used the GPCAL pipeline [15][16][17], yielding significantly better accuracy than standard AIPS LPCAL.

The VLBA BU monitoring program provided 18 epochs at 43 GHz (2015–2017) [18][19]. HSA 8 GHz observations (2023 December) combined all ten VLBA stations, phased VLA, and Effelsberg for superb sensitivity at the southern lobe [1]. PolConvert handled linear-circular feed basis conversion for Effelsberg data [20].

External Faraday Rotation Confirmation

EVPA rotations exceeding 45° with good λ2 fit (e.g., >150° rotation between 86 and 141 GHz) conclusively indicate an external Faraday screen [1][21]. Internal Faraday rotation would deviate from λ2 for rotations above ~45° [21][22]. Bayesian linear regression with LINMIX [23] was used for the power-law fitting.

Literature Context

Prior RM Measurements of 3C 84

Trippe et al. (2010, 2012) found no polarization at 81–348 GHz with IRAM PdBI, placing upper limits requiring RM dispersion ≥ 106 rad/m2, and first proposed the circumnuclear ambient medium as the Faraday screen origin [3][24]. Plambeck et al. (2014) detected 1–3% polarization at 1.3 mm with CARMA/SMA and measured RM = 8.7×105 rad/m2, attributing it to a RIAF or flattened disk within 0.4 pc [4]. Nagai et al. (2017) found RM = (3–6)×105 rad/m2 at C3 with VLBA 43 GHz [25]. Kim et al. (2019) proposed a jet boundary layer as a more natural Faraday screen [5]. Taylor (2006) measured RM = 7000 rad/m2 at the southern lobe [2].

Accretion Physics

The paper engages extensively with RIAF/ADAF theory: the foundational ADAF framework [7][26], hot accretion flow reviews [8], and the accretion state transition threshold [9]. For M87 and Sgr A*, RIAFs are the accepted Faraday screen [6][27][28][29], but 3C 84's Eddington ratio and cold gas structures challenge this assumption here [1].

AGN Environment

NGC 1275 sits at the center of the Perseus cluster cool core [30][31]. X-ray electron density profiles from Fabian et al. (2006) give ne ∝ r−0.67 on kpc scales [30], providing the outer boundary for the Bondi-sphere model. Optical emission-line filaments extending >100 kpc carry magnetic fields of ~24 μG [32][33]. The BLR disappears below LBol/LEdd ~ 0.1–1% [34], consistent with 3C 84's borderline status.

What This Paper Supports

  • Trippe (2012) suggestion that the circumnuclear ambient medium is the Faraday screen origin — now confirmed with spatially resolved VLBI data [3]
  • Taylor (2006) southern lobe RM — confirmed 20 years later and shown to lie on the same power law, demonstrating long-term stability [2]
  • Burn (1966) external rotation criterion — EVPA rotation >45° with good λ2 fit confirmed observationally [21]
  • Bondi accretion theory — free-fall density profile ne ∝ r−1.5 within the Bondi radius is a consistent model [35][36]

What This Paper Challenges

  • Plambeck (2014): The assumption that CARMA/SMA RM comes from within 0.4 pc (a RIAF or flattened disk) is challenged — the polarized emission was likely dominated by unresolved C3, not the core [1][4]
  • Kim (2019): The jet boundary layer as dominant Faraday screen is disfavored by consistently positive RM sign and spatial coherence across all components [1][5]
  • Paraschos (2024, EHT): Core RM estimate of 6×106 rad/m2 may be unreliable because unresolved ALMA polarization is dominated by downstream jet components, not the core [1][37]
  • Common Eddington ratio of 0.4%: Revised downward to ~0.04–0.09% through better SED and MBH assumptions [1][4]

Limitations

  • Single source study — generalizability to other low-luminosity AGN remains to be tested [1]
  • Magnetic field geometry (radial vs. toroidal vs. turbulent) only weakly constrained by RM profile alone [1]
  • Core polarization remains undetected — the innermost RM regime (<1 pc) is inaccessible [1]
  • Bondi-sphere model assumes spherical symmetry and steady-state accretion [35]

Faraday Rotation Measure of 3C 84 and the Circumnuclear Environment of NGC 1275

Paper Details

Authors: Park, J., Kam, M., Yi, K., Cho, I., Lee, S.-S., Byun, D.-Y., Hodgson, J. A., et al.
arXiv: 2603.16796
Submitted: 2026-03-21
Type: Observational / Multi-frequency polarimetric VLBI
Key concepts: Faraday rotation
Bondi accretion
3C 84 / NGC 1275
RIAF/ADAF models
Parsec-scale AGN jets
Key Takeaways

Main Results

  • All RM values in 3C 84 from 1–15 pc follow a single power law RM ∝ r−2.7±0.2 (Pearson r = −0.876, p = 2×10−9), unifying two decades of apparently discrepant measurements from KVN, VLBA, and HSA [1].
  • The Faraday screen is the circumnuclear ambient medium filling the Bondi sphere (r < 11.8 pc), ruling out a RIAF [15], jet boundary layer [16], and localized gas cloud [17] via spatial extent, sign stability, and cold-gas coexistence arguments [1].
  • First spatially resolved ambient B-field measurement in an elliptical galaxy: B ∝ r−1.7 to −2.2 within the Bondi radius, from ~0.1–1.5 μG at 12 pc to mG–G scales near the core, given ne ∝ r−1.5 to −2 [1].
  • The RM power law is much steeper than M87 (k ~ 1 [6]), suggesting fundamentally different circumnuclear environments between these two nearby radio galaxies [1].

Implications

This result challenges the default assumption that large RMs in low-luminosity AGN trace RIAFs or accretion disks — the ambient circumnuclear medium can dominate Faraday rotation. The spatially resolved B-field profile provides a direct observational anchor for MHD models of AGN fueling and feedback in cool-core clusters like Perseus. For galaxy evolution, understanding the magnetic environment at parsec scales constrains how AGN jets interact with and heat the surrounding medium, a critical ingredient in the feedback cycle that regulates star formation in massive ellipticals.

Other Works

Earlier RM studies of 3C 84 by Plambeck et al. (2014) with CARMA/SMA and Nagai et al. (2017) with VLBA measured individual components but could not identify a unified trend. The RIAF interpretation favored by Plambeck et al. and the jet boundary layer model of Kim et al. (2019) are both superseded by this ambient-medium picture. The steep RM ∝ r−2.7 contrasts with M87's shallow profile (Zavala & Taylor 2002), pointing to a diversity in circumnuclear magnetization among low-luminosity AGN. Trippe et al. (2012) first suggested the ambient medium as Faraday screen origin — this work provides the definitive confirmation with spatially resolved data.

Summary

This paper presents multi-frequency polarimetric VLBI observations of 3C 84 (the radio source in NGC 1275) spanning 8–141 GHz from 2015–2024, using the Korean VLBI Network (KVN), Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA), and High Sensitivity Array (HSA) [1]. The authors detect linear polarization from multiple inner jet components spanning 1–15 pc from the black hole and show that all rotation measure (RM) values follow a single power-law RM ∝ r−2.7±0.2 [1]. This unified relation is naturally explained by a common circumnuclear ambient medium within the Bondi radius (~11.8 pc) of NGC 1275, providing the first spatially resolved measurement of the ambient magnetic field in an elliptical galaxy [1].

Key Findings

Unified RM–Distance Power Law

All RM measurements of 3C 84 from 1–15 pc — spanning inner jet components C4a, C4, C3, K, and the southern lobe — follow a single power-law RM ∝ r−2.7±0.2 with Pearson r = −0.876, p = 2.1×10−9 [1]. This unifies RM measurements that had previously seemed discrepant across multiple instruments and two decades of observations [2][3][4][5]. The power-law is much steeper than the RM profile of M87 (k~1) [6].

Circumnuclear Ambient Medium as Faraday Screen

The paper establishes that the Faraday screen is the circumnuclear ambient medium filling the Bondi sphere (r < 11.8 pc) [1]. Three lines of evidence disfavor competing hypotheses:

  • RIAF disfavored: The coexistence of BLR emission lines and FFA disk absorption requires cold gas (~104 K) that would be destroyed if a RIAF extended to parsec scales [1][7][8]. The Eddington ratio (~0.04–0.09%) places 3C 84 near the accretion state transition [1][9].
  • Jet boundary layer disfavored: Consistently positive RM sign (no temporal sign reversals) contrasts with behavior expected for dynamical jet sheaths [1][5]. RM values > 105 rad/m2 are unusual for boundary layers in nearby AGN [1].
  • Gas cloud disfavored: Persistent RM after hotspot disruption and spatially extended RM across multiple components rule out a localized origin [1][4][10].

First Spatially Resolved Ambient B-Field in an Elliptical Galaxy

By combining the RM profile with electron density models (ne ∝ r−1.5 to −2), the paper constrains B ∝ r−1.7 to −2.2 within the Bondi radius [1]. At the Bondi radius (11.8 pc), B = 0.1–1.5 μG, increasing rapidly inward toward mG–G near the VLBI core [1]. Previous elliptical galaxy magnetic field measurements were galaxy-averaged or theoretical [11][12][13].

New Polarization Detections

First polarization detections from jet components C4 (emerging robustly in 2022 December at RM = 2.6–3.5×105 rad/m2) and K (RM = 0.5–0.9×105 rad/m2) [1]. The consistent ordering C4 > C3 > K within the same epochs establishes a clear distance-dependent RM gradient [1]. HSA 8 GHz confirmed the southern lobe RM (~7000 rad/m2) is unchanged from the Taylor (2006) measurement 20 years prior [2].

Methodology

Multi-Frequency Polarimetric VLBI

The KVN's simultaneous multi-frequency capability (43–141 GHz) is uniquely suited for large RM measurements because narrow frequency spacing resolves the nπ ambiguity [1][14]. An upgraded 16-Gbps OCTAD recording system (post-2022) enabled simultaneous 4-band observations [1]. D-term calibration used the GPCAL pipeline [15][16][17], yielding significantly better accuracy than standard AIPS LPCAL.

The VLBA BU monitoring program provided 18 epochs at 43 GHz (2015–2017) [18][19]. HSA 8 GHz observations (2023 December) combined all ten VLBA stations, phased VLA, and Effelsberg for superb sensitivity at the southern lobe [1]. PolConvert handled linear-circular feed basis conversion for Effelsberg data [20].

External Faraday Rotation Confirmation

EVPA rotations exceeding 45° with good λ2 fit (e.g., >150° rotation between 86 and 141 GHz) conclusively indicate an external Faraday screen [1][21]. Internal Faraday rotation would deviate from λ2 for rotations above ~45° [21][22]. Bayesian linear regression with LINMIX [23] was used for the power-law fitting.

Literature Context

Prior RM Measurements of 3C 84

Trippe et al. (2010, 2012) found no polarization at 81–348 GHz with IRAM PdBI, placing upper limits requiring RM dispersion ≥ 106 rad/m2, and first proposed the circumnuclear ambient medium as the Faraday screen origin [3][24]. Plambeck et al. (2014) detected 1–3% polarization at 1.3 mm with CARMA/SMA and measured RM = 8.7×105 rad/m2, attributing it to a RIAF or flattened disk within 0.4 pc [4]. Nagai et al. (2017) found RM = (3–6)×105 rad/m2 at C3 with VLBA 43 GHz [25]. Kim et al. (2019) proposed a jet boundary layer as a more natural Faraday screen [5]. Taylor (2006) measured RM = 7000 rad/m2 at the southern lobe [2].

Accretion Physics

The paper engages extensively with RIAF/ADAF theory: the foundational ADAF framework [7][26], hot accretion flow reviews [8], and the accretion state transition threshold [9]. For M87 and Sgr A*, RIAFs are the accepted Faraday screen [6][27][28][29], but 3C 84's Eddington ratio and cold gas structures challenge this assumption here [1].

AGN Environment

NGC 1275 sits at the center of the Perseus cluster cool core [30][31]. X-ray electron density profiles from Fabian et al. (2006) give ne ∝ r−0.67 on kpc scales [30], providing the outer boundary for the Bondi-sphere model. Optical emission-line filaments extending >100 kpc carry magnetic fields of ~24 μG [32][33]. The BLR disappears below LBol/LEdd ~ 0.1–1% [34], consistent with 3C 84's borderline status.

What This Paper Supports

  • Trippe (2012) suggestion that the circumnuclear ambient medium is the Faraday screen origin — now confirmed with spatially resolved VLBI data [3]
  • Taylor (2006) southern lobe RM — confirmed 20 years later and shown to lie on the same power law, demonstrating long-term stability [2]
  • Burn (1966) external rotation criterion — EVPA rotation >45° with good λ2 fit confirmed observationally [21]
  • Bondi accretion theory — free-fall density profile ne ∝ r−1.5 within the Bondi radius is a consistent model [35][36]

What This Paper Challenges

  • Plambeck (2014): The assumption that CARMA/SMA RM comes from within 0.4 pc (a RIAF or flattened disk) is challenged — the polarized emission was likely dominated by unresolved C3, not the core [1][4]
  • Kim (2019): The jet boundary layer as dominant Faraday screen is disfavored by consistently positive RM sign and spatial coherence across all components [1][5]
  • Paraschos (2024, EHT): Core RM estimate of 6×106 rad/m2 may be unreliable because unresolved ALMA polarization is dominated by downstream jet components, not the core [1][37]
  • Common Eddington ratio of 0.4%: Revised downward to ~0.04–0.09% through better SED and MBH assumptions [1][4]

Limitations

  • Single source study — generalizability to other low-luminosity AGN remains to be tested [1]
  • Magnetic field geometry (radial vs. toroidal vs. turbulent) only weakly constrained by RM profile alone [1]
  • Core polarization remains undetected — the innermost RM regime (<1 pc) is inaccessible [1]
  • Bondi-sphere model assumes spherical symmetry and steady-state accretion [35]

3C 84 / NGC 1275

3C 84 / NGC 1275

Type: Radio galaxy / AGN
Level: Specialist / Research
Other names: Perseus A, 3C 84, B0316+413
Host galaxy: NGC 1275 (cD galaxy, Perseus cluster BCG)
MBH: ~1.1×109 M⊙
Jet viewing angle: ~45°
Related: Faraday rotation
Bondi accretion
RIAF/ADAF models
Recollimation shocks

3C 84 is the powerful radio source associated with NGC 1275 (Perseus A), the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) at the center of the Perseus cluster. It is one of the brightest radio sources in the sky and hosts a sub-relativistic jet that has been monitored for decades with VLBI [1][2]. The jet viewing angle is approximately 45° [3][4]. The current episode of jet activity began around 2003 [5][6].

Parsec-Scale Jet Structure

VLBI monitoring reveals several distinct jet components [1][7]:

  • Core: The 43 GHz VLBI core lies within ~0.03–0.08 mas of the black hole [8][9]. Core polarization remains undetected [1].
  • C4 / C4a: Inner jet components at ~3 mas from core. First detected in polarization in 2022 December with RM = 2.6–3.5×105 rad/m2; C4a showed RM = (6.1±1.1)×105 rad/m2 [1][7].
  • C3: A hotspot region at ~4–5 mas. The bright eastern limb emerged in late 2015 [2][10] and was disrupted after 2017 May [11]. Average RM = (3.9±0.6)×105 rad/m2 from VLBA [1]; KVN values of 1.0–3.6×105 rad/m2 (2018–2024) [1].
  • K: Downstream at 5–7 mas, with the smallest RM values (0.5–0.9×105 rad/m2) [1].
  • Southern lobe: At ~15 mas (~7.5 pc). RM = 7000 rad/m2, confirmed stable over 20 years [12][1].

The jet shows evidence of instabilities and precession [13][14]. Gamma-ray flares in 2022 November–2023 January coincided with polarization emergence at C4 [15][16].

Faraday Rotation Profile

The defining observational result is that all RM measurements from 1–15 pc follow a single power-law RM ∝ r−2.7±0.2 [1]. This unifies decades of seemingly discrepant measurements:

  • IRAM PdBI non-detections at 81–348 GHz with RM dispersion ≥ 106 rad/m2 [17][18]
  • CARMA/SMA 1.3 mm detection with RM = 8.7×105 rad/m2 [19]
  • VLBA 43 GHz C3 RM = (3–6)×105 rad/m2 [2]
  • VLBA 8–22 GHz southern lobe RM = 7000 rad/m2 [12]

The Faraday screen is the circumnuclear ambient medium within the Bondi radius (11.8 pc), not a RIAF, jet boundary layer, or individual gas cloud [1].

magnetic field

The RM profile constrains B ∝ r−1.7 to −2.2 within the Bondi radius, with B = 0.1–1.5 μG at 11.8 pc increasing rapidly inward [1]. This is the first spatially resolved ambient magnetic field measurement in an elliptical galaxy [1]. Previous estimates were galaxy-averaged [20] or theoretical [21][22].

Perseus Cluster Environment

NGC 1275 hosts extensive optical emission-line filaments extending >100 kpc, dragged outward by radio bubbles and carrying magnetic fields of ~24 μG [23][24]. X-ray observations show electron density ne ∝ r−0.67 on kpc scales [25]. The interplay between AGN feedback, cold gas precipitation, and the hot ICM creates a complex multiphase environment [26][27].

Accretion State

The Eddington ratio of 3C 84 is revised from the commonly quoted 0.4% [19] down to ~0.04–0.09% [1], placing it at the boundary of the accretion state transition where BLR emission weakens and RIAF models apply [28][29]. The coexistence of broad emission lines, an FFA disk, and a parsec-scale ambient medium places interesting constraints on accretion models [1].

The Faraday screen is the circumnuclear ambient medium within the Bondi radius (11.8 pc), not a RIAF, jet boundary layer, or individual gas cloud [1].

magnetic field

The RM profile constrains B ∝ r−1.7 to −2.2 within the Bondi radius, with B = 0.1–1.5 μG at 11.8 pc increasing rapidly inward [1]. This is the first spatially resolved ambient magnetic field measurement in an elliptical galaxy [1]. Previous estimates were galaxy-averaged [20] or theoretical [21][22].

Perseus Cluster Environment

NGC 1275 hosts extensive optical emission-line filaments extending >100 kpc, dragged outward by radio bubbles and carrying magnetic fields of ~24 μG [23][24]. X-ray observations show electron density ne ∝ r−0.67 on kpc scales [25]. The interplay between AGN feedback, cold gas precipitation, and the hot ICM creates a complex multiphase environment [26][27].

Accretion State

The Eddington ratio of 3C 84 is revised from the commonly quoted 0.4% [19] down to ~0.04–0.09% [1], placing it at the boundary of the accretion state transition where BLR emission weakens and RIAF models apply [28][29]. The coexistence of broad emission lines, an FFA disk, and a parsec-scale ambient medium places interesting constraints on accretion models [1].

AGN Feedback

AGN Feedback

Type: Galaxy evolution mechanism
Level: Intermediate
Modes: Kinetic (jet/radio) & Radiative (quasar/wind)
Key environment: Cool-core galaxy clusters, massive ellipticals
Related: Perseus cluster
Bondi accretion
Jet-cocoon dynamics
3C 84 / NGC 1275 jet

AGN feedback is the process by which energy and momentum released by an active galactic nucleus regulate the gas supply of its host galaxy and surrounding environment. It is invoked as the primary mechanism preventing runaway cooling in massive galaxies and galaxy clusters, solving the "cooling flow problem" and explaining why the most massive galaxies are red and dead [1][2].

Feedback Modes

AGN feedback operates in two broad regimes [1][2]:

  • Kinetic (radio/maintenance) mode: At low accretion rates (Lbol/LEdd ≲ 1%), relativistic jets inflate cavities and radio bubbles in the surrounding hot gas, injecting mechanical energy that offsets radiative cooling. This mode operates in massive ellipticals and BCGs with hot, Bondi-fed accretion flows [1][3].
  • Radiative (quasar/wind) mode: At high accretion rates, radiation-driven winds and outflows expel gas from the host galaxy, quenching star formation. This mode operates during luminous quasar phases [2].

NGC 1275, the BCG of the Perseus cluster, accretes at Lbol/LEdd ≈ 0.04–0.09% — firmly in the kinetic feedback regime [4]. At such low Eddington ratios, the accretion flow transitions from a thin disk to a radiatively inefficient accretion flow (RIAF) [5][6][7].

Jet-Driven Cavities and Radio Bubbles

The most direct evidence for kinetic AGN feedback comes from X-ray cavities coinciding with radio lobes in cool-core clusters [1][3]. These cavities are regions of low X-ray surface brightness where the ICM has been displaced by relativistic plasma from the AGN jet [8][9].

The Perseus cluster is the textbook example: Chandra observations reveal multiple generations of cavities inflated by the 3C 84 jet, with mechanical power of ~1044–1045 erg/s — comparable to the ICM radiative cooling luminosity [8][9][10]. This energy balance constitutes the self-regulating feedback loop: cooling gas fuels accretion, accretion powers jets, jets heat the gas [1].

Beyond cavities, additional heating channels include weak shock waves driven by jet inflation, sound waves propagating through the ICM, and turbulent mixing as buoyant bubbles rise [8][1].

Heating–Cooling Balance

In cool-core clusters, the ICM cooling time within the central ~50–100 kpc falls below 109 years. Without feedback, this predicts mass deposition rates of hundreds of M⊙/yr, but X-ray spectroscopy and star formation measurements show actual rates 10–100 times lower [1][3]. The resolution is that AGN mechanical energy, distributed through cavities, shocks, and turbulence, approximately balances radiative losses [1][10].

The feedback cycle is not perfectly smooth — it involves episodic jet activity, multiphase gas condensation, and chaotic cold accretion [11][12]. Gas cooling below ~107 K precipitates into cold clouds and filaments that rain onto the central AGN, triggering new jet episodes [11][12]. In NGC 1275, this manifests as the spectacular emission-line filament network extending over 100 kpc [13][14].

Magnetic fields and Feedback

Magnetic fields play a key role in the feedback cycle. The optical filaments in NGC 1275 are threaded by ~24 μG fields that support the gas against gravitational collapse [14]. These filaments are dragged outward by rising radio bubbles, redistributing magnetic flux and cold gas through the ICM [15][16].

Faraday rotation measurements probe the magnetic field structure at all scales. Park et al. (2026) showed that the ambient magnetic field within the Bondi radius of NGC 1275 follows B ∝ r−1.7 to −2.2, providing the first spatially resolved measurement of the field connecting the accreting gas to the jet-launching region [4]. Understanding how this field threads between the accretion flow, jet, and ICM is central to completing the feedback picture.

Star Formation Quenching

On galactic scales, AGN feedback is the leading mechanism invoked to explain the red colors and low star formation rates of massive early-type galaxies [2]. Cosmological simulations require AGN feedback to reproduce the observed galaxy luminosity function, the bimodality between blue star-forming and red quiescent galaxies, and the scaling relations between black holes and their hosts [2].

In the kinetic mode, jet heating prevents the ICM from cooling and collapsing into stars — a "maintenance" mechanism that keeps massive galaxies quenched after an initial quasar-mode blowout [1]. The detailed physics of how jet energy thermalizes and couples to the gas remains an active area of research, with Perseus serving as the primary laboratory [1][8].

ATLASGAL Survey

ATLASGAL

Type:Submillimeter survey
Full name:APEX Telescope Large Area Survey of the Galaxy
Wavelength:870 μm (345 GHz)
Resolution:~18.2″
Coverage:|ℓ| ≤ 60°, |b| ≤ 1.5° (inner Galactic plane)
Clumps cataloged:~8000
Related: Dust
Molecular clouds
IRDCs

ATLASGAL (APEX Telescope Large Area Survey of the Galaxy) is an unbiased 870 μm continuum survey of the inner Galactic plane conducted with the LABOCA bolometer array on the 12-m APEX telescope in Chile[1]. It provides the most comprehensive census of dense dust clumps in the Milky Way, including the progenitors of star formation across all evolutionary stages.

Survey Products

The ATLASGAL compact source catalog contains ~8000 dense clumps with measured velocities, kinematic distances, luminosities, and masses[2]. At 870 μm, the dust emission is optically thin, making it an excellent tracer of total gas column density and mass.

Role in Star Formation Studies

ATLASGAL clumps serve as the parent sample for targeted follow-up studies of magnetic fields, kinematics, and fragmentation in high-mass star-forming regions. For G351.77-0.53, ATLASGAL identified the embedded clumps (c1–c6) along the filament, with clump c1 hosting the massive protocluster IRAS 17233-3606[3].

Baryon Fraction in Galaxy Clusters

Baryon Fraction

Type:Cosmological diagnostic
Cosmic value:Ωb/Ωm ≈ 0.157
Related: Mass Estimation
SZ Effect
Coma Cluster

The baryon fraction fb in galaxy clusters — the ratio of baryonic (gas + stellar) mass to total mass — is expected to approximate the cosmic value Ωb/Ωm because clusters are large enough to retain a representative sample of the universe's matter content [1].

Coma Cluster Measurement

Pedratti et al. (2026) derive fb = 0.15 ± 0.02 at r = 2.12 Mpc for the Coma Cluster, consistent with the cosmological value [2][1]. The gas mass profile is estimated from XMM-Newton electron density measurements [3].

Blue Monsters

Blue Monsters

Type:Galaxy population / evolutionary phase
Level:Specialist
Epoch:z > 10 (cosmic dawn)
Defining feature:Blue UV slopes (βUV < −2.5), dust-free after outflow clearing
Duration:~80% of galaxy lifetime
Related: Red monsters
Cosmic dawn
High-redshift galaxies
UV slope
Stellar feedback

Blue monsters are UV-bright, dust-free galaxies at z > 10 that represent the dominant observed population at cosmic dawn. In the Ferrara+2024 radiative-feedback framework[1], blue monsters are the evolutionary successors of red monsters: galaxies that have transitioned from a dust-obscured, rapidly star-forming phase to a UV-luminous phase after radiation-driven outflows have expelled their gas and dust.

Evolutionary Context

In the Ferrara+2024 model[1], the blue monster phase follows the red monster phase in a cycle regulated by radiative feedback:

  1. During the red monster phase (~20% of lifetime), rapid star formation builds up stellar mass in a compact, dust-enshrouded environment.
  2. Radiation pressure from the growing stellar population (and possibly an accreting black hole) drives outflows that expel gas and dust.
  3. With dust cleared, the galaxy enters the blue monster phase (~80% of lifetime), becoming UV-bright with extremely blue UV slopes (βUV < −2.5).

Blue monsters dominate observational samples at z > 10 because they spend most of their time in this UV-bright state[1]. Their prevalence explains the canonical MUV–βUV relation at these redshifts, which shows a tight correlation between UV magnitude and blue UV slope[2].

Observational Properties

The majority of spectroscopically confirmed galaxies at z > 10 exhibit blue monster characteristics[3][4]:

  • Blue UV slopes: βUV < −2 to −3, consistent with young, metal-poor, dust-free stellar populations[2][5]
  • High UV luminosities: Exceeding pre-JWST model predictions, suggesting high star formation efficiencies[3][4]
  • Strong emission lines: Lyman-α, C III], and other UV lines indicating vigorous ionizing photon production[6]
  • Compact morphologies: Effective radii of order 100–500 pc[3]

Implications for Reionization

Blue monsters are prime candidates for driving cosmic reionization, as their UV-bright, dust-free state maximizes the escape of ionizing photons into the intergalactic medium. However, if the preceding red monster phase is common, the total ionizing photon budget at z > 10 must account for both visible (blue) and obscured (red) star formation[1]. The spectroscopic confirmation of EGS-z11-R0 as a red monster at z = 11.45[7] demonstrates that the transition between these phases was already occurring at the earliest observed epochs.

  1. During the red monster phase (~20% of lifetime), rapid star formation builds up stellar mass in a compact, dust-enshrouded environment.
  2. Radiation pressure from the growing stellar population (and possibly an accreting black hole) drives outflows that expel gas and dust.
  3. With dust cleared, the galaxy enters the blue monster phase (~80% of lifetime), becoming UV-bright with extremely blue UV slopes (βUV < −2.5).

Blue monsters dominate observational samples at z > 10 because they spend most of their time in this UV-bright state[1]. Their prevalence explains the canonical MUV–βUV relation at these redshifts, which shows a tight correlation between UV magnitude and blue UV slope[2].

Observational Properties

The majority of spectroscopically confirmed galaxies at z > 10 exhibit blue monster characteristics[3][4]:

  • Blue UV slopes: βUV < −2 to −3, consistent with young, metal-poor, dust-free stellar populations[2][5]
  • High UV luminosities: Exceeding pre-JWST model predictions, suggesting high star formation efficiencies[3][4]
  • Strong emission lines: Lyman-α, C III], and other UV lines indicating vigorous ionizing photon production[6]
  • Compact morphologies: Effective radii of order 100–500 pc[3]

Implications for Reionization

Blue monsters are prime candidates for driving cosmic reionization, as their UV-bright, dust-free state maximizes the escape of ionizing photons into the intergalactic medium. However, if the preceding red monster phase is common, the total ionizing photon budget at z > 10 must account for both visible (blue) and obscured (red) star formation[1]. The spectroscopic confirmation of EGS-z11-R0 as a red monster at z = 11.45[7] demonstrates that the transition between these phases was already occurring at the earliest observed epochs.

Bondi Accretion

Bondi Accretion

Type: Accretion mechanism
Level: Intermediate
First described: 1952 (Hermann Bondi)
Related: RIAF/ADAF models
Faraday rotation
3C 84 / NGC 1275

Bondi accretion describes the spherically symmetric accretion of gas onto a compact object from a uniform medium at infinity. The Bondi radius rB = GM/cs2 defines the gravitational sphere of influence, within which gas is gravitationally captured and falls approximately in free fall [1]. In AGN, the Bondi radius sets the outer boundary of the circumnuclear accretion environment and is a key scale for understanding the transition from galaxy-scale gas to accretion-flow-scale physics [1][2].

Theory

The Bondi (1952) solution assumes a non-rotating, steady-state, spherically symmetric gas flow with adiabatic equation of state [1]. The accretion rate is:

ṀB = 4πλ (GM)2 ρ∞ / cs,∞3

where λ is a dimensionless parameter depending on the adiabatic index, M is the central mass, ρ∞ and cs,∞ are the density and sound speed at infinity [1]. Within the Bondi radius, gas falls at the free-fall velocity v ∝ r−0.5, and for adiabatic accretion the electron density follows ne ∝ r−1.5 [1][3].

Application to NGC 1275

For NGC 1275 (the host galaxy of 3C 84), the adopted black hole mass MBH = 1.1×109 M⊙ [4][5] and an ambient sound speed inferred from X-ray observations yield a Bondi radius of ~11.8 pc [3][6]. Fujita & Nagai (2016) used this framework to model the electron density and predict Faraday rotation from the infalling gas [3].

Park et al. (2026) showed that the RM profile of 3C 84 from 1–15 pc follows RM ∝ r−2.7, naturally explained by a circumnuclear medium filling the Bondi sphere with ne ∝ r−1.5 to −2 and B ∝ r−1.7 to −2.2 [6]. This represents the first observational application of Bondi-sphere gas properties via spatially resolved RM.

Limitations of the Bondi Model

  • Spherical symmetry: Real accretion involves rotation, jets, and outflows that break spherical symmetry [1]
  • Steady-state assumption: AGN feedback, jet-driven cavities, and cold gas precipitation can modulate the accretion rate on short timescales [7][8]
  • Multiphase medium: Cold gas filaments, molecular clouds, and hot ICM coexist within the Bondi sphere in Perseus [9][10], complicating the single-phase assumption
  • Magnetic fields: Not included in the original Bondi solution but may affect the accretion rate and flow geometry [6]

Bullet Dwarf Scenario

Bullet Dwarf Scenario

Type:Galaxy formation mechanism
First proposed:Silk (2019) [1]
Analog:Bullet Cluster (Clowe et al. 2006) [2]
Related: Dark-matter-free galaxies
Ultra-diffuse galaxies
NFW Profile

Definition

The bullet dwarf scenario proposes that dark-matter-free galaxies form when two gas-rich dwarf galaxies collide at high speed (~300 km/s), physically separating their baryonic gas from dark matter halos [1]. The gas, stripped from its dark matter, collapses to form new galaxies that contain stars but little or no dark matter — a dwarf-scale analog of the Bullet Cluster [2].

Physical Mechanism

Foundational: Collisionless vs. collisional matter

Dark matter is collisionless: dark matter particles pass through each other during a collision. Gas, however, is collisional: it shocks, compresses, and can be separated from dark matter during high-speed encounters [2]. This fundamental difference enables the physical separation of baryonic and dark components during galaxy-galaxy collisions.

Intermediate: Collision dynamics

In the bullet dwarf model, two gas-rich galaxies pass through each other at high velocity. The dark matter halos pass through unimpeded, while the gas from both galaxies shocks, decelerates, and is left behind along the collision axis [1][3]. This gas then fragments and forms new galaxies — producing a linear trail of objects along the collision trajectory [3].

Specialist: Predictions and confirmations

The scenario makes several specific, testable predictions:

  • Linear alignment: Newly-formed galaxies should be arranged in a line along the collision axis [3] — confirmed by the discovery of the NGC 1052 trail [4]
  • Kinematic connection: Trail members should have velocities linearly increasing with position [3] — confirmed by Keim et al. (2025) [5]
  • Dark matter deficiency: Trail galaxies should lack dark matter [1] — confirmed for DF2 [6], DF4 [7], and DF9 [8]
  • Luminous globular clusters: High-pressure gas conditions during collision should produce unusually massive star clusters [9] — confirmed [10][11]
  • Large sizes: Galaxies formed from expanding gas should be unusually diffuse [12] — confirmed
  • Monochromatic GC colors: Single-burst star formation should produce uniform cluster colors [13] — confirmed

Simulations

Shin et al. (2020) [3] produced the first hydrodynamical simulations of the bullet dwarf scenario, demonstrating that a high-speed collision can produce a trail of dark-matter-free objects. Lee et al. (2021) [9] showed the scenario naturally produces luminous globular clusters. Lee et al. (2024) [14] performed simulations specific to the NGC 1052 system, demonstrating that trail galaxies can survive tidal disruption in the group environment.

Dispute: Survivability of trail galaxies

Ogiya (2022) [15] argued that dark-matter-free galaxies would be quickly disrupted by tidal forces in the NGC 1052 group.

Lee et al. (2024) [14] showed survivability is possible under realistic tidal field conditions. The detection of DF9 with properties consistent with DF2/DF4 provides empirical evidence [8].

Alternative Formation Scenarios

Several alternatives have been proposed for dark-matter-free galaxy formation:

  • Tidal dwarf galaxies: Formed from debris of massive galaxy mergers [16]; cannot explain the trail’s kinematic structure or luminous GCs
  • Quasar-driven outflows: Gas expelled by AGN activity [17]; does not predict the observed linear alignment
  • Tidal stripping of streams: Linear substructures from infalling material [18]; does not explain GC properties

Only the bullet dwarf scenario explains the full combination of trail alignment, dark matter deficiency, luminous GCs, large sizes, and monochromatic cluster colors [8].

Butcher-Oemler Effect

Butcher-Oemler Effect

Type:Observational phenomenon
First described:Butcher & Oemler (1978) [1]
Related: RS / GV / BC Classification
Ram-Pressure Stripping

The Butcher-Oemler effect is the observation that galaxy clusters at intermediate redshifts (z ~ 0.3–0.5) contain a significantly higher fraction of blue, star-forming galaxies than nearby clusters [1]. This provided early evidence that galaxy populations in clusters evolve with cosmic time, with environmental processes progressively quenching star formation.

Connection to Modern Understanding

The Butcher-Oemler effect is now understood within the framework of environmental quenching: infalling blue cloud galaxies are transformed by mechanisms including ram-pressure stripping, strangulation, and harassment. The population-dependent orbital anisotropy seen in the Coma Cluster [2] represents the z ≈ 0 end-point of this evolutionary process.

Collisional Ring Galaxies

Collisional Ring Galaxies

Type:Intermediate concept
Formation:Near head-on galaxy collisions producing expanding density waves
Lifetime:≤0.5 Gyr
Prevalence:~1% of all ring galaxies; ~130 known at low z
Related: Galaxy mergers
Star formation
Dynamical stellar displacement
Type Ia supernovae

Collisional ring galaxies (CRGs) are a class of peculiar galaxies formed when a companion galaxy passes nearly head-on through the disk of a target galaxy, producing a collisionally driven density wave that propagates radially outward as an expanding ring[1][2]. Unlike resonance rings (formed by bar-driven orbital resonances) or polar rings (formed by accretion onto orthogonal orbits), CRGs arise from impulsive gravitational perturbations during "bullseye" collisions[3][4].

Formation and Properties

CRGs require a specific encounter geometry: the intruder galaxy must pass close to the center of the target disk[1]. The strongest ring features arise in major mergers (comparable-mass encounters) during the brief merging phase[5][6]. The expanding density wave sweeps up disk gas, compressing it and triggering enhanced star formation at the ring front[7][8][9]. Rings can extend tens of kpc from the host galaxy center, with expansion velocities of 50–150 km/s[10][6].

CRGs have short lifetimes (≤0.5 Gyr from formation to dissolution)[10][11][6], making them intrinsically rare. Only ~130 have been identified at low redshift (z ~ 0.002–0.09), representing ~1% of all ring galaxies[12]. However, ring morphologies have been detected at z > 1–3 in deep surveys[13][14].

Dual Stellar Populations

A key property of CRGs is that the expanding density wave not only triggers new star formation but also dynamically displaces pre-existing old stellar populations from the disk[15][16]. This produces a characteristic dual population within the ring: young stars formed in situ alongside old stars stripped from the parent galaxy. In "Pika's Halo," deep Ca H&K absorption (old stars) coexists with bright emission lines (ongoing SF at SFR ~ 0.91 M☉/yr)[16].

CRGs as Supernova Factories

CRGs host elevated transient rates due to their combination of enhanced star formation and displaced old stellar populations[16]. A crossmatch of the Madore et al. (2009) low-z CRG catalog with TNS reveals 15 transients in 11 CRG systems (8% of the catalog), with 3 systems hosting supernova siblings[16]. The displaced old populations provide a channel for Type Ia SNe at large offsets from the host nucleus[16].

Detection Methods

CRGs have been identified through visual inspection of peculiar galaxy catalogs[17][18], citizen science projects[19], and increasingly through machine learning classification[20][21]. At higher redshifts, surface brightness dimming makes faint ring structures difficult to detect, potentially causing hostless SN misclassification[16].

Why This Matters

CRGs serve as unique laboratories for studying the interplay between gravitational dynamics and star formation during galaxy mergers. Their expanding rings provide dynamically constrained age estimates, enabling studies of stellar population evolution with known formation timescales. The discovery that CRGs can produce large-offset Type Ia SNe via dynamical displacement[16] has implications for supernova cosmology and the hostless SN problem.
